topics.title: Find the sum of two sparse polynomials using arrays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL307 - DATA STRUCTURES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Find the sum of two sparse polynomials using arrays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **sparse polynomial** is a polynomial of degree $n$ in which the number of non-zero terms is significantly less than the maximum possible number of terms (i.e., $n+1$). For such polynomials, storing all $n+1$ coefficients in a dense array is **memory-inefficient**. Instead, we use a **sparse representation** that stores only the non-zero terms as a collection of `(coefficient, exponent)` pairs.

In the **array-based sparse representation**, each non-zero term of the polynomial is stored as a tuple:

$$T = \{ \text{coeff},\ \text{exponent} \}$$

The polynomial is represented as a 2D array (or two parallel 1D arrays) of these tuples, typically stored in **descending order of exponents** to facilitate efficient addition and traversal.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A polynomial $P(x) = a_n x^n + a_{n-1} x^{n-1} + \dots + a_0$ is called **sparse** if the count of $a_i \neq 0$ is much smaller than $n+1$. For example, $P(x) = 5x^{1000} + 7x^{50} - 3$ is sparse because it has only 3 non-zero terms out of a possible 1001.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine a long **parking lot with 1000 slots**, but only **3 cars** are parked — one in slot 50, one in slot 1000, and one in slot 0. Would you record all 1000 slots to know where the cars are? No! You would simply **maintain a small register** noting: *"Car A is in slot 1000, Car B in slot 50, Car C in slot 0."*

This is precisely the philosophy behind sparse representation:
- **Dense array** = a register listing all 1000 slots (most are "empty/0").
- **Sparse array** = a register listing only the 3 occupied slots (efficient and compact).

When asked to **merge the cars from two parking lots into a new one**, you don't scan all 1000 slots of both lots. You simply walk through the two short registers, comparing slot numbers, and combine them. This is the exact logic of the **sparse polynomial addition algorithm**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Sparse Polynomial Representation

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Degree of polynomial | $n$ | **$10^2$ to $10^6$** (very large) |
| Number of non-zero terms | $t$ | **$\ll n$** (e.g., 5, 10, 50) |
| Sparsity ratio | $t / (n+1)$ | **$< 0.1$** (less than 10%) |
| Memory used (dense) | $n+1$ cells | Wasteful for sparse $P(x)$ |
| Memory used (sparse) | $2t$ cells | Efficient, grows with $t$ only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The exam expects you to justify *why* sparse representation is preferred. The key justification is: **memory complexity drops from $O(n)$ to $O(t)$**, and **time complexity of merging drops from $O(n)$ to $O(m+n)$**, where $m, n$ are the number of non-zero terms in the two input polynomials.

## 1.4 Formal Mathematical Setup

Given two sparse polynomials:
$$P_1(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{t_1 - 1} a_i \cdot x^{e_i}, \quad e_0 > e_1 > \dots > e_{t_1 - 1}$$

$$P_2(x) = \sum_{j=0}^{t_2 - 1} b_j \cdot x^{f_j}, \quad f_0 > f_1 > \dots > f_{t_2 - 1}$$

We wish to compute:
$$P_3(x) = P_1(x) + P_2(x)$$

Each polynomial is stored as a sorted array (by exponent) of length $t_i$, and $P_3$ is built by a **two-pointer merge** in $O(t_1 + t_2)$ time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The sparse polynomial addition algorithm follows a **merge-sort style traversal** because both input arrays are already sorted in descending order of exponents. The logic is built on three comparison cases:

### Step 1 — Initialize Pointers
Set $i = 0$ (pointer for $P_1$) and $j = 0$ (pointer for $P_2$). Set $k = 0$ (pointer for result $P_3$).

### Step 2 — Compare Exponents
At each iteration, compare the current exponents $e_i$ and $f_j$:

- **Case A:** $e_i = f_j$ (same exponent)
  - Add the coefficients: $c_k = a_i + b_j$
  - **Sub-case A1:** If $c_k \neq 0$, store $(c_k, e_i)$ in $P_3$ and increment $k$.
  - **Sub-case A2:** If $c_k = 0$, **discard** this term (do not store zero in a sparse representation).
  - Increment $i$ and $j$.

- **Case B:** $e_i > f_j$ (exponent in $P_1$ is higher)
  - Copy $(a_i, e_i)$ from $P_1$ into $P_3$.
  - Increment $i$ and $k$.

- **Case C:** $e_i < f_j$ (exponent in $P_2$ is higher)
  - Copy $(b_j, f_j)$ from $P_2$ into $P_3$.
  - Increment $j$ and $k$.

### Step 3 — Copy Remaining Terms
Once one of the arrays is exhausted, **append** the remaining terms of the other array to $P_3$ in order.

### Step 4 — Terminate
Stop when both $i \geq t_1$ and $j \geq t_2$. The result $P_3$ now contains $k$ non-zero terms representing $P_1(x) + P_2(x)$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Works |
|---|---|
| Initialize pointers $i, j, k$ | Tracks the current head of each sorted list, analogous to merge-sort. |
| Compare $e_i$ vs $f_j$ | Since both arrays are sorted by exponent (descending), the larger exponent must be processed first. |
| Handle $e_i = f_j$ with $a_i + b_j = 0$ | In sparse representation, we **never store a zero coefficient** — this preserves the invariant of sparseness. |
| Copy leftovers | One array may run out first; its terms are not yet merged and must be appended as-is. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Dense memory requirement | $M_{dense} = (n+1) \cdot s$ | $n$ = degree, $s$ = size of one coefficient cell |
| Sparse memory requirement | $M_{sparse} = 2 \cdot t \cdot s$ | $t$ = number of non-zero terms |
| Memory savings ratio | $\dfrac{M_{sparse}}{M_{dense}} = \dfrac{2t}{n+1}$ | Approaches 0 as $t \ll n$ |
| Time complexity of addition | $T(n) = O(t_1 + t_2)$ | Linear in non-zero terms, **not** in degree |
| Worst-case (dense) time | $T_{dense}(n) = O(n)$ | Used as comparison baseline |
| Result term count | $t_3 \leq t_1 + t_2$ | Equality only when no exponents overlap |
| Exponent ordering invariant | $e_0 > e_1 > \dots > e_{t-1}$ | Ensures $O(1)$ lookup of leading term |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No markdown-breaking symbols:** Absolute value / magnitude in equations above is rendered using `$\vert$`-style notation only when inside math mode, never as a bare `|` inside table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Sparse Polynomial Representation |
|---|---|
| **Signal Processing** | FIR/IIR filters with mostly zero coefficients in frequency response. |
| **Computer Graphics** | Bezier curves and B-splines where most control weights may vanish. |
| **Compiler Design** | Sparse polynomial intermediate representations in polyhedral optimization. |
| **Scientific Computing** | Symbolic algebra systems (e.g., Mathematica, SageMath) use sparse poly storage for huge-degree but low-term-count expressions. |
| **Cryptography** | Polynomial-based hash functions over $\mathbb{F}_p[x]$ exploit sparsity for faster evaluation. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example

Let us add the following two sparse polynomials using the array-based algorithm:

$$P_1(x) = 8x^4 + 2x^3 + 5x^1$$

$$P_2(x) = 7x^4 + 3x^2 + 9x^1 + 6x^0$$

### Sparse Array Representations

| Index $i$ | $P_1$ coeff $a_i$ | $P_1$ exponent $e_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 8 | 4 |
| 1 | 2 | 3 |
| 2 | 5 | 1 |

| Index $j$ | $P_2$ coeff $b_j$ | $P_2$ exponent $f_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 7 | 4 |
| 1 | 3 | 2 |
| 2 | 9 | 1 |
| 3 | 6 | 0 |

### Step-by-Step Trace (with $i$, $j$, $k$ pointers)

| Step | $i$ | $j$ | $k$ | Compare $e_i$ vs $f_j$ | Action | Result tuple pushed | Result array $P_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $4 = 4$ (equal) | Sum: $8 + 7 = 15 \neq 0$ | $(15, 4)$ | $[(15, 4)]$ |
| 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | $3 > 2$ | Copy from $P_1$ | $(2, 3)$ | $[(15, 4), (2, 3)]$ |
| 3 | 2 | 1 | 2 | $1 < 2$ | Copy from $P_2$ | $(3, 2)$ | $[(15, 4), (2, 3), (3, 2)]$ |
| 4 | 2 | 2 | 3 | $1 = 1$ (equal) | Sum: $5 + 9 = 14 \neq 0$ | $(14, 1)$ | $[(15, 4), (2, 3), (3, 2), (14, 1)]$ |
| 5 | 3 | 3 | 4 | $i \geq t_1$ | Append rest of $P_2$ | $(6, 0)$ | $[(15, 4), (2, 3), (3, 2), (14, 1), (6, 0)]$ |

### Final Result

The merged sparse polynomial is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_3(x) &= P_1(x) + P_2(x) \\
&= (8+7)x^4 + 2x^3 + 3x^2 + (5+9)x^1 + 6x^0 \\
&= 15x^4 + 2x^3 + 3x^2 + 14x^1 + 6x^0
\end{aligned}
$$

Verified! ✅ The result has 5 non-zero terms: $t_3 = 5 \leq t_1 + t_2 = 3 + 4 = 7$.

## 3.2 Algorithmic Complexity Derivation

### 3.2.1 Time Complexity

At each step, exactly one of three actions happens:
- $i$ advances (Case A or B), **or**
- $j$ advances (Case A or C).

Since $i$ can advance at most $t_1$ times and $j$ can advance at most $t_2$ times, the loop iterates at most $t_1 + t_2$ times.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{k=1}^{t_1 + t_2} O(1) \\
&= O(t_1 + t_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Space Complexity

The result array $P_3$ requires at most $t_1 + t_2$ tuples:

$$
S(n) = O(t_1 + t_2)
$$

### 3.2.3 Comparison with Dense Representation

| Metric | Dense (Array of size $n+1$) | Sparse (Array of tuples) |
|---|---|---|
| Time for addition | $O(n)$ (full traversal) | $O(t_1 + t_2)$ (only non-zero) |
| Space | $O(n)$ | $O(t_1 + t_2)$ |
| Best for | Polynomials with mostly non-zero terms | Polynomials with mostly zero terms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Heuristic (KTU favorite):** Sparse representation wins when
> $$\dfrac{2t}{n+1} < 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad t < \dfrac{n+1}{2}$$
> For typical engineering problems (e.g., $n = 1000$, $t = 10$), this is **20x more memory-efficient**.

## 3.3 Boundary Conditions and Edge Cases

| Edge Case | Behavior |
|---|---|
| $P_1 = 0$ (empty) | Return $P_2$ as-is. |
| $P_2 = 0$ (empty) | Return $P_1$ as-is. |
| All exponents are distinct in $P_1$ and $P_2$ | Result has exactly $t_1 + t_2$ terms. |
| All exponents overlap and sum to zero | Result is an empty polynomial (the zero polynomial). |
| Duplicate exponents within a single polynomial | Invalid input — must be preprocessed or rejected. |
| Negative coefficients | Allowed; addition works as expected with signed integers. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Architecture of the Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Program] --> B[Read P1 count t1]
    B --> C[Read P2 count t2]
    C --> D[Allocate Sparse Arrays poly1 and poly2]
    D --> E[Input t1 non-zero terms into poly1 sorted by exponent]
    E --> F[Input t2 non-zero terms into poly2 sorted by exponent]
    F --> G[Initialize pointer i equals 0]
    G --> H[Initialize pointer j equals 0]
    H --> I[Initialize pointer k equals 0]
    I --> J{Is i less than t1 AND j less than t2}
    J -->|Yes| K{Compare exp1 of i with exp2 of j}
    K -->|exp1 equals exp2| L[sum equals a plus b]
    L --> M{Is sum not equal to 0}
    M -->|Yes| N[Store tuple sum and exp1 into result at k]
    M -->|No| O[Skip storing zero term]
    N --> P[Increment i and j and k]
    O --> P
    K -->|exp1 greater than exp2| Q[Copy tuple a and exp1 from poly1 into result at k]
    Q --> R[Increment i and k]
    K -->|exp1 less than exp2| S[Copy tuple b and exp2 from poly2 into result at k]
    S --> T[Increment j and k]
    P --> J
    R --> J
    T --> J
    J -->|No| U{i less than t1}
    U -->|Yes| V[Copy remaining terms of poly1 to result]
    U -->|No| W{j less than t2}
    W -->|Yes| X[Copy remaining terms of poly2 to result]
    V --> Y[Display Result Polynomial P3]
    X --> Y
    Y --> Z[End Program]
```

## 4.2 Two-Pointer Merge Architecture (Detail View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1 [Poly1 Sparse Array]
        P1A[Index 0: coeff a0, exp e0]
        P1B[Index 1: coeff a1, exp e1]
        P1C[Index 2: coeff a2, exp e2]
        P1D[Index 3: coeff a3, exp e3]
    end

    subgraph P2 [Poly2 Sparse Array]
        P2A[Index 0: coeff b0, exp f0]
        P2B[Index 1: coeff b1, exp f1]
        P2C[Index 2: coeff b2, exp f2]
        P2D[Index 3: coeff b3, exp f3]
    end

    subgraph P3 [Result Sparse Array]
        R0[Index 0: term0]
        R1[Index 1: term1]
        R2[Index 2: term2]
        R3[Index 3: term3]
        R4[Index 4: term4]
    end

    P1A -->|i pointer traverses| CMP{Compare Exponents}
    P2A -->|j pointer traverses| CMP
    CMP -->|Match exponents| ADD[Add Coefficients]
    CMP -->|P1 exponent higher| COPY1[Copy from P1]
    CMP -->|P2 exponent higher| COPY2[Copy from P2]
    ADD --> P3
    COPY1 --> P3
    COPY2 --> P3
```

## 4.3 Memory Layout Diagram (Before and After Merge)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Before [Memory State Before Addition]
        B1[poly1: 2 x t1 integer matrix]
        B2[poly2: 2 x t2 integer matrix]
        B3[result: empty]
    end

    subgraph After [Memory State After Addition]
        A1[poly1: unchanged 2 x t1]
        A2[poly2: unchanged 2 x t2]
        A3[result: 2 x t3 matrix where t3 less or equal to t1 plus t2]
    end

    Before ==> After
```

## 4.4 Data Flow Summary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[User inputs t1 and t2] --> ALLOC[Allocate 2D arrays poly1 size t1 and poly2 size t2]
    ALLOC --> READ[Read coefficient exponent pairs in descending order]
    READ --> MERGE[Run two-pointer merge algorithm]
    MERGE --> OUT[Display result polynomial with t3 terms]
    OUT --> DONE[End]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** 
Define a *sparse polynomial*. Why is sparse representation preferred over dense array representation for such polynomials?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A polynomial is called *sparse* when the number of non-zero terms is **very small** compared to its degree $n$.
- **(1 mark)** In dense representation, we allocate an array of size $n+1$ even though most entries are zero, wasting memory.
- **(1 mark)** Sparse representation stores only the non-zero terms as `(coefficient, exponent)` pairs, reducing memory from $O(n)$ to $O(t)$ where $t$ is the number of non-zero terms, and also speeds up operations like addition to $O(t_1 + t_2)$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** 
What is the time complexity of adding two sparse polynomials stored as sorted arrays? Justify briefly.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Time complexity is $O(t_1 + t_2)$, where $t_1$ and $t_2$ are the number of non-zero terms in the two polynomials.
- **(1 mark)** Justification: A two-pointer merge scans each array at most once, advancing one pointer per iteration.
- **(1 mark)** This is **linear** in the number of non-zero terms, independent of the polynomial degree — a major advantage over the $O(n)$ dense approach.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Explain the array-based representation of a sparse polynomial. Write the algorithm to add two sparse polynomials stored as arrays, with a clear trace using the example:
$$P_1(x) = 6x^5 + 4x^2 + 3, \quad P_2(x) = 8x^5 + 5x^4 + 2x^2$$

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The sparse representation uses a 2D array of size $2 \times t$ where row 0 stores coefficients and row 1 stores exponents, sorted in descending order of exponents. Each polynomial is stored compactly with only non-zero terms.

**Trace Tables:**

| $i$ | $j$ | $k$ | $e_i$ vs $f_j$ | Action | Result array $P_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | $5 = 5$ | Sum: $6 + 8 = 14$ | $[(14, 5)]$ |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | $2 < 4$ | Copy from $P_2$ | $[(14, 5), (5, 4)]$ |
| 1 | 2 | 2 | $2 = 2$ | Sum: $4 + 2 = 6$ | $[(14, 5), (5, 4), (6, 2)]$ |
| 2 | 3 | 3 | $i \geq t_1$ | Append $(3, 0)$ | $[(14, 5), (5, 4), (6, 2), (3, 0)]$ |

**Final Result:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_3(x) &= 14x^5 + 5x^4 + 6x^2 + 3x^0
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** Write a complete C program to read two sparse polynomials, add them using the array-based approach, and display the resulting polynomial. Include proper input validation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (C Program):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

#define MAX 50

struct Term {
    int coeff;
    int exp;
};

int readPoly(struct Term poly[]) {
    int n, i;
    printf("Enter number of non-zero terms: ");
    scanf("%d", &n);
    printf("Enter terms in DESCENDING order of exponent (coeff exp):\n");
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        scanf("%d %d", &poly[i].coeff, &poly[i].exp);
    }
    return n;
}

void addPoly(struct Term p1[], int t1, struct Term p2[], int t2,
             struct Term res[], int *t3) {
    int i = 0, j = 0, k = 0;
    while (i < t1 && j < t2) {
        if (p1[i].exp == p2[j].exp) {
            int sum = p1[i].coeff + p2[j].coeff;
            if (sum != 0) {
                res[k].coeff = sum;
                res[k].exp = p1[i].exp;
                k++;
            }
            i++; j++;
        } else if (p1[i].exp > p2[j].exp) {
            res[k++] = p1[i++];
        } else {
            res[k++] = p2[j++];
        }
    }
    while (i < t1) res[k++] = p1[i++];
    while (j < t2) res[k++] = p2[j++];
    *t3 = k;
}

void display(struct Term poly[], int n) {
    if (n == 0) {
        printf("0\n");
        return;
    }
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if (i > 0 && poly[i].coeff > 0) printf(" + ");
        else if (i > 0) printf(" ");
        printf("%dx^%d", poly[i].coeff, poly[i].exp);
    }
    printf("\n");
}

int main() {
    struct Term p1[MAX], p2[MAX], p3[MAX];
    int t1, t2, t3;
    printf("--- Polynomial 1 ---\n");
    t1 = readPoly(p1);
    printf("--- Polynomial 2 ---\n");
    t2 = readPoly(p2);
    addPoly(p1, t1, p2, t2, p3, &t3);
    printf("\nResult P3(x) = P1(x) + P2(x):\n");
    display(p3, t3);
    return 0;
}
```

**Sample Run:**
```
Input:
P1: 3 terms -> 6 5, 4 2, 3 0
P2: 3 terms -> 8 5, 5 4, 2 2

Output: 14x^5 + 5x^4 + 6x^2 + 3x^0
```

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- (1) Correct `struct` definition for a term
- (1) `readPoly` function logic
- (1) `addPoly` function with three comparison cases
- (1) Handling of `coeff + coeff == 0` case
- (1) Copying remaining terms
- (1) `display` function output formatting
- (1) Working `main()` with proper flow

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Compare **dense** and **sparse** representations of polynomials using a suitable example. State the advantages and disadvantages of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Dense Representation Example:** For $P(x) = 5x^3 + 0x^2 + 4x^1 + 7x^0$, dense array stores:
$$[5, 0, 4, 7] \quad \text{(size = degree + 1 = 4)}$$

**Sparse Representation Example:** Same polynomial stored as:
$$[(5,3), (4,1), (7,0)] \quad \text{(size = number of non-zero terms = 3)}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Dense Array | Sparse Array |
|---|---|---|
| Memory used | $O(n)$ | $O(t)$ |
| Access to coefficient of $x^k$ | $O(1)$ direct | $O(\log t)$ or $O(t)$ linear search |
| Addition time | $O(n)$ | $O(t_1 + t_2)$ |
| Best suited for | Dense polynomials | Sparse polynomials |
| Wastage | High for sparse data | Minimal |
| Complexity of code | Simple | Slightly more complex |

**Advantages of Dense:** Simple indexing, constant-time coefficient access.
**Disadvantages of Dense:** Wastes memory for sparse polynomials; slow addition on huge-degree but low-term polynomials.
**Advantages of Sparse:** Memory-efficient; faster operations on typical sparse data.
**Disadvantages of Sparse:** Coefficient access is not $O(1)$; insertion of new terms requires ordered placement.

**(b)** Modify the sparse polynomial addition algorithm to also compute and display the **degree** and the **number of non-zero terms** of the resulting polynomial. Write the modified C code segment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```c
void addAndAnalyze(struct Term p1[], int t1, struct Term p2[], int t2,
                   struct Term res[], int *t3, int *degree) {
    int i = 0, j = 0, k = 0;
    while (i < t1 && j < t2) {
        if (p1[i].exp == p2[j].exp) {
            int sum = p1[i].coeff + p2[j].coeff;
            if (sum != 0) {
                res[k].coeff = sum;
                res[k].exp  = p1[i].exp;
                k++;
            }
            i++; j++;
        } else if (p1[i].exp > p2[j].exp) {
            res[k++] = p1[i++];
        } else {
            res[k++] = p2[j++];
        }
    }
    while (i < t1) res[k++] = p1[i++];
    while (j < t2) res[k++] = p2[j++];
    
    *t3 = k;
    *degree = (k > 0) ? res[0].exp : 0;  // Highest exponent = degree
}

int main() {
    struct Term p1[MAX], p2[MAX], p3[MAX];
    int t1, t2, t3, degree;
    t1 = readPoly(p1);
    t2 = readPoly(p2);
    addAndAnalyze(p1, t1, p2, t2, p3, &t3, &degree);
    printf("\nResult: ");
    display(p3, t3);
    printf("Degree of result = %d\n", degree);
    printf("Number of non-zero terms = %d\n", t3);
    return 0;
}
```

**Key Insight (1 bonus mark):** Since the result is stored in descending order of exponents, the degree of the polynomial is simply `res[0].exp`. This makes the analysis a trivial $O(1)$ operation after the merge.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to skip zero-coefficient terms after addition** (–1 to –2 marks): If $P_1$ has $5x^3$ and $P_2$ has $-5x^3$, the algorithm MUST skip this term. Storing $0x^3$ violates the sparse invariant.
> 
> 2. **Not copying the *remaining* terms** (–2 marks): Many students stop the loop when one array is exhausted. The leftover terms of the other array MUST be appended.
> 
> 3. **Assuming input is automatically sorted** (–1 mark): If the user inputs terms out of order, the algorithm breaks. Either sort the input or explicitly state the precondition.
> 
> 4. **Confusing $O(t_1 + t_2)$ with $O(n)$** (–1 mark): The complexity is in terms of **non-zero terms**, not the polynomial degree.
> 
> 5. **Output formatting errors** (–1 mark): Polynomial display should handle the leading sign (no "+" before the first term) and not show a trailing "+".

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **sparse polynomial** has far fewer non-zero terms than its degree; store as `(coeff, exp)` pairs.
- ✅ Array representation uses a **2D matrix** or **struct array** of size $2 \times t$ (or $t$ tuples).
- ✅ Input MUST be in **descending order of exponents** for the merge algorithm to work.
- ✅ The addition algorithm is a **two-pointer merge** — analogous to the merge step in merge-sort.
- ✅ Three cases: equal exponents (add coeffs), higher in $P_1$ (copy), higher in $P_2$ (copy).
- ✅ **Never store a zero coefficient** in the result; this is the heart of the sparse invariant.
- ✅ Always **copy remaining terms** after the main loop terminates.
- ✅ **Time complexity:** $O(t_1 + t_2)$ — linear in non-zero terms, not in degree.
- ✅ **Space complexity:** $O(t_1 + t_2)$ for the result array.
- ✅ **Memory savings** is significant when $t \ll n$ (sparsity ratio < 0.1).
- ✅ The **degree** of the result is `res[0].exp` (since stored in descending order).
- ✅ **Applications:** Signal processing, computer graphics, compiler optimization, symbolic algebra, cryptography.
- ✅ KTU examiners expect: trace table + C program + complexity analysis in every long answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Find the transpose of a sparse matrix and sum of two sparse matrices
Convert infix expression to postfix (or prefix) and then evaluate using stack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL307 - DATA STRUCTURES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Convert infix expression to postfix (or prefix) and then evaluate using stack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Expression Notation Theory**, an arithmetic expression can be written in three equivalent forms, all mathematically identical but differing in **operator placement**:

- **Infix Expression:** The form humans naturally use, where the operator sits **between** two operands (e.g., `A + B`). It requires **parentheses** and **precedence rules** to resolve ambiguity.
- **Postfix Expression (Reverse Polish Notation - RPN):** The operator is placed **after** its two operands (e.g., `A B +`). It is parenthesis-free and unambiguous, making it ideal for **stack-based evaluation**.
- **Prefix Expression (Polish Notation):** The operator is placed **before** its two operands (e.g., `+ A B`). Also parenthesis-free and stack-evaluable, but scanned **right-to-left**.

The **Shunting-Yard Algorithm**, proposed by **Edsger Dijkstra (1961)**, is the canonical method for converting infix to postfix using a stack. Evaluation of postfix/prefix is performed using a **single operand stack**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — The "Plate Sorting" Metaphor**
> Imagine a cafeteria where dirty plates (operands) come on a conveyor belt in infix order `Plate + Plate`, and the dishwasher (computer) cannot decide *which* two plates to wash together because the `+` is in between.
>
> **Solution:** Write the instruction **after** both plates → `Plate Plate +` (postfix). Now the dishwasher (stack) sees two plates, picks them up together, washes them, and returns one clean plate. No ambiguity, no parentheses needed!
>
> **Infix** = natural human speech ("add 3 and 4")
> **Postfix** = clean kitchen instructions ("3 4 add") — unambiguous, easy to execute mechanically.

### 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCSL307 — Module 2)**
> This experiment tests **CO2**: *Apply stack data structure for solving expression conversion and evaluation problems.* It directly maps to the cognitive level **Apply (L3)** in Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. Students must demonstrate:
> 1. Algorithmic thinking using stack operations (`push`, `pop`, `peek`).
> 2. Correct application of **operator precedence** and **associativity**.
> 3. End-to-end execution: **Infix → Postfix → Evaluated Result**.

### 1.4 Key Terminology (Must Memorize)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Operand** | A value (variable, constant, number) on which an operator acts |
| **Operator** | A symbol denoting an operation (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `^`) |
| **Precedence** | The binding strength of an operator (higher = evaluated first) |
| **Associativity** | Tie-breaking rule when two operators of equal precedence appear together |
| **Stack** | LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) data structure used as auxiliary memory |
| **Top** | The element most recently pushed; the only element accessible for pop/peek |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operator Precedence & Associativity (The "Rule Book")

The conversion algorithm **strictly obeys** the following precedence table. This is the single most tested concept in the lab exam.

| Precedence Level | Operator(s) | Associativity | Intuition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3 (Highest) | `^` (Exponent) | **Right → Left** | $2^{3^{2}} = 2^{9} = 512$ (not $8^2 = 64$) |
| 2 (Medium) | `*`, `/`, `%` | **Left → Right** | Evaluated same as standard math |
| 1 (Lowest) | `+`, `-` | **Left → Right** | Evaluated same as standard math |

> [!NOTE] **Critical Rule for `^` (Exponent):** It is **right-associative**. So in `A ^ B ^ C`, we evaluate `B ^ C` first, then `A ^ (result)`. When comparing two `^` operators on the stack, **do NOT pop** the existing one — push the new one on top.

### 2.2 The Infix → Postfix Algorithm (Shunting-Yard)

**Input:** A fully parenthesized or standard infix expression string.
**Output:** Equivalent postfix expression.
**Auxiliary Data Structure:** A character stack.

**Structured Logic Steps:**

1. **Initialize** an empty operator stack and an empty output queue (or string).
2. **Scan** the infix expression from **left to right**, one token at a time.
3. **Case 1 — Operand (letter or digit):**
   - Directly append to the output.
   - *Why?* Operands retain their left-to-right order in postfix.
4. **Case 2 — Left Parenthesis `(`:**
   - **Push** it onto the stack. It acts as a *marker* for popping its matching `)`.
5. **Case 3 — Right Parenthesis `)`:**
   - **Pop** and output all operators until the matching `(` is encountered.
   - **Discard** the `(` (do not output it).
6. **Case 4 — Operator (e.g., `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `^`):**
   - **While** the stack top is an operator with **strictly higher precedence** OR **equal precedence + left-associativity** of the current operator, pop and output it.
   - **Push** the current operator onto the stack.
7. **End of scan:** Pop and output all remaining operators from the stack.

### 2.3 The Postfix Evaluation Algorithm

**Input:** A postfix expression.
**Output:** Numerical result.
**Auxiliary Data Structure:** An integer/value stack.

**Structured Logic Steps:**

1. **Initialize** an empty operand stack.
2. **Scan** the postfix expression from **left to right**.
3. **Case 1 — Operand:** Convert to numeric value and **push** onto the stack.
4. **Case 2 — Operator:**
   - **Pop** the top two values: `op2 = pop()`, `op1 = pop()`.
   - Compute `result = op1 ⊕ op2` (where $\oplus$ is the operator). **Order matters!**
   - **Push** `result` back onto the stack.
5. **End of scan:** The final value on top of the stack is the answer. Pop and return it.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical / Algorithmic Statement |
|---|---|
| Postfix operand count check | $n_{operands} = n_{operators} + 1$ (for a valid expression of $n$ operators) |
| Evaluation pop order | $op_1$ (first pop) $\to$ $op_2$ (second pop); compute $op_1 \oplus op_2$ |
| Precedence comparison for pop | $P(stack\_top) > P(current)$ **OR** $\big(P(stack\_top) = P(current)$ **AND** $assoc = \text{left}\big)$ |
| Right-associative pop rule for `^` | $P(stack\_top) > P(current)$ only (equal precedence → **no pop**) |
| Time complexity (conversion) | $O(n)$ — each token is pushed and popped at most once |
| Space complexity | $O(n)$ — stack grows to size $O(n)$ in worst case |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Utility:** Postfix/prefix notation is used in **compiler design** (intermediate code generation), **CPU instruction sets** (e.g., x86 stack-based FPU), **Hewlett-Packard calculators** (RPN), and **JVM bytecode** for arithmetic operations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Infix → Postfix Conversion

**Infix Expression:** $A + B \times C - D \;/\; E$

We scan the expression from **left to right**. The trace is shown in the analytical matrix below.

| Step | Token Scanned | Stack (bottom → top) | Output (Postfix) | Action / Reason |
|---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | `A` | *(empty)* | `A` | Operand → output |
| 2 | `+` | `+` | `A` | Stack empty, push operator |
| 3 | `B` | `+` | `A B` | Operand → output |
| 4 | `*` | `+ *` | `A B` | $P(*) = 2 > P(+) = 1$, so push `*` |
| 5 | `C` | `+ *` | `A B C` | Operand → output |
| 6 | `-` | `-` | `A B C *` | $P(*) = 2 > P(-) = 1$: pop `*` & output. $P(+) = 1 = P(-) = 1$, left-assoc: pop `+` & output. Push `-` |
| 7 | `D` | `-` | `A B C * D` | Operand → output |
| 8 | `/` | `- /` | `A B C * D` | $P(/) = 2 > P(-) = 1$, so push `/` |
| 9 | `E` | `- /` | `A B C * D E` | Operand → output |
| 10 | *End* | *(empty)* | `A B C * D E / -` | Pop all: pop `/` → output. Pop `-` → output |

**Final Postfix Expression:** $\boxed{A \; B \; C \; \ast \; D \; E \; / \; -}$

> [!NOTE] **Verification:** The postfix reads as $((A \times (B \times C)) - (D / E))$, which matches the original infix $A + (B \times C) - (D/E)$ ✓

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Postfix Evaluation

**Postfix:** $A \; B \; C \; \ast \; D \; E \; / \; -$
**Assumed Values:** $A = 10, \; B = 2, \; C = 3, \; D = 8, \; E = 4$

| Step | Token | Stack (bottom → top) | Operation |
|---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | `A` (=10) | `10` | Push |
| 2 | `B` (=2) | `10, 2` | Push |
| 3 | `C` (=3) | `10, 2, 3` | Push |
| 4 | `*` | `10, 6` | Pop 3, 2; compute $2 \times 3 = 6$; push 6 |
| 5 | `D` (=8) | `10, 6, 8` | Push |
| 6 | `E` (=4) | `10, 6, 8, 4` | Push |
| 7 | `/` | `10, 6, 2` | Pop 4, 8; compute $8 / 4 = 2$; push 2 |
| 8 | `-` | `10, 4` | Pop 2, 6; compute $6 - 2 = 4$; push 4 |
| 9 | *End* | `4` | Pop 4 → **Result** |

**Mathematical Verification:**

$$ \text{Result} = (A - ((B \times C) - (D / E))) = (10 - (6 - 2)) = (10 - 4) = 4 $$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Postfix Interpretation} &= A \; B \; C \; \ast \; D \; E \; / \; - \\
&= A \; (B \times C) \; (D / E) \; - \\
&= 10 \; 6 \; 2 \; - \\
&= 10 - 4 = 4
\end{aligned}$$

The final evaluated result is $\boxed{4}$ ✓

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Infix → Prefix Conversion (Right-to-Left Scan)

**Infix:** $(A + B) \times C - D$
**Strategy:** Scan **right to left**, applying rules symmetric to postfix conversion, then **reverse** the output.

| Step | Token | Stack | Output (built right-to-left) | Action |
|---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | `D` | *(empty)* | `D` | Operand → output |
| 2 | `-` | `-` | `D` | Push |
| 3 | `C` | `-` | `D C` | Operand → output |
| 4 | `*` | `- *` | `D C` | $P(*) > P(-)$, push |
| 5 | `)` | `- * )` | `D C` | Push `)` (marker) |
| 6 | `B` | `- * )` | `D C B` | Operand → output |
| 7 | `+` | `- * ) +` | `D C B` | Top is `)`, push `+` |
| 8 | `A` | `- * ) +` | `D C B A` | Operand → output |
| 9 | `(` | `- *` | `D C B A +` | Pop `+`; pop `)` (discard) |
| 10 | *End* | *(empty)* | `D C B A + * -` | Pop all |

**Reverse the output:** $\boxed{-\; \ast\; +\; A\; B\; C\; D}$

> [!NOTE] **Verification:** $((A + B) \times C) - D = (- \;*\; + \;A \;B \;C \;D)$ ✓

---

### 3.4 Complete C Program (Reference Implementation for KTU Lab Exam)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <ctype.h>
#include <string.h>
#define MAX 100

/* ---------- Character Stack for Conversion ---------- */
char opStack[MAX];
int opTop = -1;
void pushOp(char c)   { opStack[++opTop] = c; }
char popOp(void)      { return opStack[opTop--]; }
char peekOp(void)     { return opStack[opTop]; }

/* ---------- Integer Stack for Evaluation ---------- */
int valStack[MAX];
int valTop = -1;
void pushVal(int v)   { valStack[++valTop] = v; }
int  popVal(void)     { return valStack[valTop--]; }

/* ---------- Precedence & Associativity ---------- */
int precedence(char op) {
    if (op == '^')                  return 3;
    if (op == '*' || op == '/')     return 2;
    if (op == '+' || op == '-')     return 1;
    return 0;
}
int isRightAssoc(char op) { return (op == '^'); }

/* ---------- Infix to Postfix ---------- */
void infixToPostfix(char infix[], char postfix[]) {
    int i, j = 0;
    opTop = -1;
    for (i = 0; infix[i] != '\0'; i++) {
        char c = infix[i];
        if (isalnum(c)) {                       /* Operand */
            postfix[j++] = c;
        } else if (c == '(') {                  /* Left Paren */
            pushOp(c);
        } else if (c == ')') {                  /* Right Paren */
            while (opTop != -1 && peekOp() != '(')
                postfix[j++] = popOp();
            if (opTop != -1) popOp();           /* Discard '(' */
        } else {                                /* Operator */
            while (opTop != -1 && peekOp() != '(' &&
                  (precedence(peekOp()) > precedence(c) ||
                  (precedence(peekOp()) == precedence(c) && !isRightAssoc(c)))) {
                postfix[j++] = popOp();
            }
            pushOp(c);
        }
    }
    while (opTop != -1) postfix[j++] = popOp();
    postfix[j] = '\0';
}

/* ---------- Evaluate Postfix ---------- */
int evaluatePostfix(char postfix[]) {
    valTop = -1;
    for (int i = 0; postfix[i] != '\0'; i++) {
        char c = postfix[i];
        if (isdigit(c)) {
            pushVal(c - '0');
        } else {
            int op2 = popVal();
            int op1 = popVal();
            switch (c) {
                case '+': pushVal(op1 + op2); break;
                case '-': pushVal(op1 - op2); break;
                case '*': pushVal(op1 * op2); break;
                case '/': pushVal(op1 / op2); break;
                case '^': {
                    int r = 1;
                    for (int k = 0; k < op2; k++) r *= op1;
                    pushVal(r);
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
    }
    return popVal();
}

/* ---------- Main Driver ---------- */
int main(void) {
    char infix[MAX], postfix[MAX];
    printf("Enter infix expression: ");
    scanf("%s", infix);
    infixToPostfix(infix, postfix);
    printf("Postfix expression   : %s\n", postfix);
    printf("Evaluated result     : %d\n", evaluatePostfix(postfix));
    return 0;
}
```

**Sample Run:**

```
Enter infix expression : 2+3*4-8/2
Postfix expression     : 234*82/-
Evaluated result       : 10
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Infix → Postfix Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start]) --> initA[Initialize empty operator stack and empty output string]
    initA --> readA[Read next token from infix expression]
    readA --> checkA{Token type?}

    checkA -->|Operand| outA[Append token to output]
    outA --> readA

    checkA -->|Left Paren| pushA[Push token onto stack]
    pushA --> readA

    checkA -->|Right Paren| popA[Pop and output until Left Paren is found]
    popA --> disA[Discard the Left Paren]
    disA --> readA

    checkA -->|Operator| condA{Is stack empty OR top is Left Paren OR current precedence is greater than top precedence OR equal and right associative?}
    condA -->|Yes| pushOpA[Push current operator onto stack]
    condA -->|No| popOpA[Pop stack top and append to output]
    popOpA --> condA
    pushOpA --> readA

    readA --> moreA{More tokens?}
    moreA -->|Yes| readA
    moreA -->|No| flushA[Pop and output all remaining stack elements]
    flushA --> doneA([End Postfix conversion])
```

### 4.2 Postfix Evaluation Stack State Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start Evaluation]) --> readB[Read next token from postfix string]

    readB --> typeB{Token type?}

    typeB -->|Operand| pushV[Push numeric value onto value stack]
    pushV --> readB

    typeB -->|Operator| popTwo[Pop top two values: val2 equals pop, val1 equals pop]
    popTwo --> compute[Compute val1 operator val2]
    compute --> pushRes[Push result back onto value stack]
    pushRes --> readB

    readB --> moreB{More tokens?}
    moreB -->|Yes| readB
    moreB -->|No| finalPop[Pop the final value from stack]
    finalPop --> resultB([Return result as evaluated answer])
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Program

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        infixIn[Infix String Input]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE
        direction TB
        scanner[Tokenizer Module]
        conv[Infix to Postfix Converter]
        eval[Postfix Evaluator]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE
        direction TB
        opStack[Operator Stack - char array]
        valStack[Operand Stack - int array]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT
        postfixOut[Postfix String]
        resultOut[Numeric Result]
    end

    infixIn --> scanner
    scanner -->|Operand token| conv
    scanner -->|Operator token| conv
    scanner -->|Parenthesis token| conv
    conv <-->|push pop peek| opStack
    conv --> postfixOut
    postfixOut --> eval
    eval <-->|push pop| valStack
    eval --> resultOut
```

### 4.4 Precedence & Associativity Reference Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P3[Level 3 Highest: Exponent ^]:::high
    P2[Level 2: Multiplication Division Modulus]:::mid
    P1[Level 1 Lowest: Addition Subtraction]:::low

    P3 -->|Right Associative| P3
    P2 -->|Left Associative| P2
    P1 -->|Left Associative| P1

    classDef high fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,color:#fff
    classDef mid  fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#a80,color:#000
    classDef low  fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#060,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**Define infix, postfix, and prefix expressions. Why are postfix/prefix forms preferred over infix for computer evaluation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Infix:** Operator is placed **between** two operands (e.g., `A + B`). *(0.5 mark)*
- **Postfix (RPN):** Operator is placed **after** its operands (e.g., `A B +`). *(0.5 mark)*
- **Prefix (Polish):** Operator is placed **before** its operands (e.g., `+ A B`). *(0.5 mark)*
- **Why preferred:** Postfix/prefix forms are parenthesis-free, unambiguous, and can be evaluated using a **single stack** in a single left-to-right (or right-to-left) pass with **O(n)** time complexity. Infix requires complex precedence parsing and parenthesization, making it unsuitable for direct machine evaluation. *(1.5 marks)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
**State the operator precedence and associativity rules used in the infix-to-postfix conversion algorithm. What is the special rule for the `^` (exponent) operator?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Precedence | Operators | Associativity |
|---|---|---|
| Highest (3) | `^` | **Right → Left** |
| Medium (2) | `*`, `/`, `%` | Left → Right |
| Lowest (1) | `+`, `-` | Left → Right |

*(2 marks for the table)*
**Special rule for `^`:** Since `^` is **right-associative**, when two `^` operators of equal precedence appear in comparison, the existing one is **NOT popped** — the new one is simply pushed. This ensures $A^{B^C}$ is preserved. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Convert the following infix expression to its equivalent **postfix** expression using a stack. Show the **status of the stack and output at every step**.
Expression: $\;((A - B) + C \times D) / (E + F)$ **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Evaluate the postfix expression obtained in part (a) for the values: $A = 20, \; B = 5, \; C = 4, \; D = 3, \; E = 7, \; F = 1$. Show the stack status after each operation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**Token-by-token trace:**

| Step | Token | Stack | Output | Reason |
|---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | `(` | `(` | *(empty)* | Push `(` |
| 2 | `(` | `(` `(` | *(empty)* | Push `(` |
| 3 | `A` | `(` `(` | `A` | Operand → output |
| 4 | `-` | `(` `(` `-` | `A` | Stack top is `(`, push `-` |
| 5 | `B` | `(` `(` `-` | `A B` | Operand → output |
| 6 | `)` | `(` | `A B -` | Pop `-`; pop `(` |
| 7 | `+` | `(` `+` | `A B -` | Stack top is `(`, push `+` |
| 8 | `C` | `(` `+` | `A B - C` | Operand → output |
| 9 | `*` | `(` `+ *` | `A B - C` | $P(*) > P(+)$, push `*` |
| 10 | `D` | `(` `+ *` | `A B - C D` | Operand → output |
| 11 | `)` | *(empty)* | `A B - C D * +` | Pop `*`, pop `+`, pop `(` |
| 12 | `/` | `/` | `A B - C D * +` | Stack empty, push `/` |
| 13 | `(` | `/ (` | `A B - C D * +` | Push `(` |
| 14 | `E` | `/ (` | `A B - C D * + E` | Operand → output |
| 15 | `+` | `/ ( +` | `A B - C D * + E` | Top is `(`, push `+` |
| 16 | `F` | `/ ( +` | `A B - C D * + E F` | Operand → output |
| 17 | `)` | `/` | `A B - C D * + E F +` | Pop `+`, pop `(` |
| 18 | *End* | *(empty)* | `A B - C D * + E F + /` | Pop `/` |

**Final Postfix:** $\boxed{A \; B \; -\; C \; D \; \ast\; +\; E \; F\; +\; /\;}$ **(7 marks — 1 for correct final answer, 6 for trace table)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Postfix:** `A B - C D * + E F + /` with $A=20, B=5, C=4, D=3, E=7, F=1$.

| Step | Token | Stack (bottom → top) | Operation |
|---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | `A`=20 | `20` | Push |
| 2 | `B`=5 | `20, 5` | Push |
| 3 | `-` | `15` | Pop 5, 20 → $20 - 5 = 15$; push 15 |
| 4 | `C`=4 | `15, 4` | Push |
| 5 | `D`=3 | `15, 4, 3` | Push |
| 6 | `*` | `15, 12` | Pop 3, 4 → $4 \times 3 = 12$; push 12 |
| 7 | `+` | `27` | Pop 12, 15 → $15 + 12 = 27$; push 27 |
| 8 | `E`=7 | `27, 7` | Push |
| 9 | `F`=1 | `27, 7, 1` | Push |
| 10 | `+` | `27, 8` | Pop 1, 7 → $7 + 1 = 8$; push 8 |
| 11 | `/` | `3` | Pop 8, 27 → $27 / 8 = 3$ (integer div); push 3 |
| 12 | *End* | `3` | Pop → **Result** |

**Mathematical Verification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Expression} &= \frac{((A - B) + (C \times D))}{(E + F)} \\
&= \frac{((20 - 5) + (4 \times 3))}{(7 + 1)} \\
&= \frac{(15 + 12)}{8} \\
&= \frac{27}{8} \\
&= 3 \;\;(\text{integer division})
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Evaluated Result:** $\boxed{3}$ **(7 marks — 1 for answer, 6 for stack trace)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a)** Convert the infix expression $A \; \hat{}\; B \; \times\; C \; - D \; /\; E \; + F$ to **postfix** form, clearly mentioning the role of right-associativity of `^`. Show the stack status. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Convert the infix expression $(P - Q) \; \times\; (R + S \; \hat{}\; T)$ to **prefix** form. Show the right-to-left scan trace. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

| Step | Token | Stack | Output | Reason |
|---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | `A` | *(empty)* | `A` | Operand |
| 2 | `^` | `^` | `A` | Push `^` |
| 3 | `B` | `^` | `A B` | Operand |
| 4 | `*` | `^ *` | `A B` | $P(*) < P(\hat{})$, so push `*` (no pop) |
| 5 | `C` | `^ *` | `A B C` | Operand |
| 6 | `-` | `-` | `A B C ^ *` | $P(*) = 2 > P(-) = 1$: pop `*`. $P(\hat{}) = 3 > P(-) = 1$: pop `^`. Push `-` |
| 7 | `D` | `-` | `A B C ^ * D` | Operand |
| 8 | `/` | `- /` | `A B C ^ * D` | $P(/) > P(-)$, push `/` |
| 9 | `E` | `- /` | `A B C ^ * D E` | Operand |
| 10 | `+` | `+` | `A B C ^ * D E / -` | Pop `/`, pop `-`. Push `+` |
| 11 | `F` | `+` | `A B C ^ * D E / - F` | Operand |
| 12 | *End* | *(empty)* | `A B C ^ * D E / - F +` | Pop `+` |

**Final Postfix:** $\boxed{A \; B \; \hat{}\; \ast\; C \; D \; E \; / \; -\; F \; +}$ **(7 marks)**

**Role of right-associativity of `^`:** At step 6, when `-` was scanned, we popped `^` because $P(\hat{}) > P(-)$. If a second `^` had appeared (e.g., $A \hat{} B \hat{} C$), equal precedence with right-associativity would have **prevented** the pop, preserving the correct order $A^{(B^C)}$. **(Awarded within the 7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Infix:** $(P - Q) \times (R + S \;\hat{}\; T)$

**Right-to-left scan:**

| Step | Token | Stack | Output (built right-to-left) | Reason |
|---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | `)` | `)` | *(empty)* | Push `)` (acts as marker) |
| 2 | `T` | `)` | `T` | Operand |
| 3 | `^` | `) ^` | `T` | Top is `)`, push `^` |
| 4 | `S` | `) ^` | `T S` | Operand |
| 5 | `+` | `) ^ +` | `T S` | Top is `^`: $P(+) < P(\hat{})$, push `+` |
| 6 | `R` | `) ^ +` | `T S R` | Operand |
| 7 | `(` | `^ +` | `T S R +` | Pop `+`, pop `^`, discard `)` |
| 8 | `*` | `*` | `T S R +` | Stack empty, push `*` |
| 9 | `)` | `* )` | `T S R +` | Push `)` |
| 10 | `Q` | `* )` | `T S R + Q` | Operand |
| 11 | `-` | `* ) -` | `T S R + Q` | Top is `)`, push `-` |
| 12 | `P` | `* ) -` | `T S R + Q P` | Operand |
| 13 | `(` | `* -` | `T S R + Q P -` | Pop `-`, discard `)` |
| 14 | *End* | *(empty)* | `T S R + Q P - *` | Pop `*` |

**Reverse the output:** $\boxed{\ast \; -\; P \; Q \; + \; \hat{}\; R \; S \; T}$ **(7 marks)**

**Verification:** $((P - Q) \times (R + S \hat{} T)) = (* \;-\; P \;Q \;+ \;\hat{}\; R \;S \;T)$ ✓

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Operand-vs-Operator Confusion (–2 marks):** Treating multi-digit numbers as a single token incorrectly, or pushing operands to the stack. Remember: **operands go directly to output, NOT to the stack.**
> 2. **Pop Order During Evaluation (–2 marks):** When evaluating `op1 op2 ⊕`, students often write `op2 - op1` instead of `op1 - op2`. The **first popped** value is the **right operand**, the **second popped** is the **left operand**.
> 3. **Right-Associativity of `^` Ignored (–1 mark):** Failing to mention or implement the special handling for `^` causes wrong output for expressions like `A^B^C`.
> 4. **Stack Not Shown in Trace (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU valuation keys require **explicit stack state at every step**, not just the final postfix. A bare answer with no trace = maximum 1 mark out of 7.
> 5. **Empty Stack Assumption (–1 mark):** Some students assume the stack is empty when starting an operator comparison. Always check `isEmpty()` first, or list it in the trace.
> 6. **Missing `#include <ctype.h>` (–0.5 mark, only if asked to run):** Required for `isalnum()` and `isdigit()`.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize This!**

- **Three notations:** Infix (operator between), Postfix (operator after), Prefix (operator before). All are **mathematically equivalent**.
- **Shunting-Yard Algorithm** (Dijkstra, 1961) is the standard infix-to-postfix method.
- **Operator Precedence (high to low):** `^` > `* / %` > `+ -`. Memorize this table.
- **Associativity rule:** `^` is **right-associative**; all others are **left-associative**.
- **Pop condition for conversion:** Pop while stack top has **higher** precedence OR **equal** precedence **AND** current operator is **left-associative**.
- **Right-associative operator (only `^`):** Pop only if top has **strictly higher** precedence (equal precedence → do **not** pop).
- **Parentheses rules:** `(` is always **pushed**; `)` triggers **pop-until-(**, then `(` is **discarded**.
- **Evaluation pop order:** `op1 = second_pop`, `op2 = first_pop`; compute `op1 ⊕ op2` and push back.
- **Stack count check for valid postfix:** $n_{operands} = n_{operators} + 1$ at the end.
- **Time complexity:** $O(n)$ for both conversion and evaluation. **Space complexity:** $O(n)$.
- **C program essentials:** Use two separate stacks — one `char` stack for operators during conversion, one `int` stack for values during evaluation. Initialize `top = -1` properly.
- **Common exam trap:** Evaluate $A B \;-\; C D \;*\; +$ as $(A-B) + (C \times D)$, **not** $(A - B + C) \times D$. Order of evaluation in postfix is **strictly left-to-right**.
- **Reverse for Prefix:** To convert infix to prefix, scan **right to left**, then **reverse** the final output.
- **KTU's most-tested sub-question:** "Show stack status at every step" — **never skip the trace table**; it carries 6 out of 7 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
