```python
        # 3) REPLACEMENTS
        for i in range(len(word)):
            for c in letters:
                candidates.add(word[:i] + c + word[i+1:])

        # 4) INSERTIONS
        for i in range(len(word) + 1):
            for c in letters:
                candidates.add(word[:i] + c + word[i:])

        return candidates

    def suggest(self, word):
        """Returns list of valid dictionary words at edit distance 1."""
        candidates = self.edit_distance_1(word)
        return [w for w in candidates if self.ht.lookup(w)]

    def check_document(self, text):
        """Validates an entire document word by word."""
        tokens = re.findall(r"[A-Za-z']+", text)
        results = []
        for tok in tokens:
            w = tok.lower()
            if self.check_word(w):
                results.append((tok, "OK", []))
            else:
                suggestions = self.suggest(w)
                results.append((tok, "MISSPELLED", suggestions[:5]))
        return results


# ---- 3. Driver / Demonstration Code ----
if __name__ == "__main__":
    sc = SpellChecker("dictionary.txt")

    # Interactive mode
    print("=== KTU Hash-Based Spell Checker ===")
    print("Type a word (or 'exit' to quit):")
    while True:
        word = input("> ").strip()
        if word.lower() == "exit":
            break
        if sc.check_word(word):
            print(f"✔  '{word}' is CORRECT.\n")
        else:
            sugs = sc.suggest(word)
            if sugs:
                print(f"✘  '{word}' is MISSPELLED.  Suggestions: {sugs}\n")
            else:
                print(f"✘  '{word}' is MISSPELLED.  No suggestions found.\n")
```

### 3.5 Sample Output Trace

```
=== KTU Hash-Based Spell Checker ===
Type a word (or 'exit' to quit):
> speling
✘  'speling' is MISSPELLED.  Suggestions: ['spelling', 'spieling']

> hello
✔  'hello' is CORRECT.

> has
✔  'has' is CORRECT.

> algoritm
✘  'algoritm' is MISSPELLED.  Suggestions: ['algorithm']
```

### 3.6 Time-Complexity Audit (Viva Favourite)

| Operation | Time Complexity | Reason |
|-----------|-----------------|--------|
| Dictionary insertion | $O(L)$ per word | $L$ = word length |
| Single word lookup | $O(L + \alpha)$ ≈ $O(L)$ | Hash + chain traversal |
| Suggestion generation (ED-1) | $O(L \cdot 26 \cdot L) = O(26 L^{2})$ | 26 letters × 4 edit types |
| Suggestion lookup | $O(26 L^{2} \cdot L) = O(26 L^{3})$ | Each candidate checked |
| Full document of $N$ words | $O(N \cdot L)$ | Linear in document size |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture of the Hash-Based Spell Checker

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Input Word] --> B[Normalize to Lowercase]
    B --> C[Compute Hash Index h]
    C --> D{Bucket Found?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Traverse Chain & Compare]
    E --> F{Match Exists?}
    F -- Yes --> G[VALID - Word is Correct]
    F -- No --> H[INVALID - Misspelled]
    H --> I[Generate Edit-Distance-1 Candidates]
    I --> J[Hash Lookup Each Candidate]
    J --> K{Valid Dictionary Matches?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Return Suggestions]
    K -- No --> M[Return No Suggestions]
    M --> N[End]
    L --> N[End]
    G --> N[End]
    D -- No --> O[Bucket Empty - Misspelled]
    O --> I
```

### 4.2 Hash Table Structure (Separate Chaining)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph H0["Bucket 0"]
        H0N["(empty)"]
    end
    subgraph H1["Bucket 1"]
        H1A["head"] --> H1B["hello"] --> H1C["hash"] --> H1D["NULL"]
    end
    subgraph H2["Bucket 2"]
        H2A["head"] --> H2B["world"] --> H2C["NULL"]
    end
    subgraph H3["Bucket 3"]
        H3A["(empty)"]
    end
    subgraph H4["Bucket 4"]
        H4A["head"] --> H4B["python"] --> H4C["program"] --> H4D["NULL"]
    end
    subgraph H5["Bucket 5"]
        H5A["(empty)"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Edit-Distance-1 Candidate Generation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Input Word: cat] --> D[Deletions]
    W --> T[Transpositions]
    W --> R[Replacements]
    W --> I[Insertions]

    D --> D1["at, ct, ca"]
    T --> T1["act, cta"]
    R --> R1["a 26, b 26, ..., z 26"]
    I --> I1["acat, bcat, ..., cact, ..., zat, ..., caat"]

    D1 --> U[Union Set of Candidates]
    T1 --> U
    R1 --> U
    I1 --> U
    U --> H[Hash Lookup Each Candidate]
    H --> Out[Valid Dictionary Words = Suggestions]
```

### 4.4 Data Flow Diagram — Document Checking Mode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Doc[Input Document .txt] --> Tok[Tokenizer regex]
    Tok --> Loop{For each word}
    Loop --> H1[Compute Hash]
    H1 --> LT[Hash Table Lookup]
    LT --> Decision{Word in Dictionary?}
    Decision -- Yes --> OK[Mark as OK]
    Decision -- No --> Sug[Generate Suggestions]
    Sug --> Out[Output Report Table]
    OK --> Out
    Out --> Loop
    Loop -- All words done --> Final[Final Report Displayed]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Define a hash function. List any two hash functions suitable for strings with a brief justification for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A hash function $h(k)$ maps a key $k$ to an integer in the range $[0, m-1]$ where $m$ is the table size. For string keys, two common hash functions are:
1. **Polynomial Rolling Hash** — $h = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} \text{ord}(k[i]) \cdot p^{i} \bmod m$ where $p$ is a prime. It distributes characters with positional weighting, reducing clustering.
2. **DJB2 Hash** — $h = ((h \ll 5) + h) + c$. It is simple, fast, and produces good distribution for short ASCII strings.

*(Award 1 mark for definition + 1 mark per hash function with justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Series 2]**
*What is the average-case time complexity of hash table lookup, and how does it benefit a spell checker checking a 10,000-word document?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Average-case lookup in a well-designed hash table with load factor $\alpha \leq 0.75$ is $O(1 + \alpha) \approx O(1)$. 
For a 10,000-word document, total validation time is $O(N) = 10{,}000$ operations (essentially 1 operation per word) — versus $O(N \cdot D) = 10{,}000 \times 100{,}000$ operations in linear search, where $D$ is the dictionary size. This makes hash-based spell checkers **approximately 100,000× faster** for large dictionaries.

*(Award 1.5 marks for complexity statement + 1.5 marks for application reasoning.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: *Either-Or* model. Either attempt **Question A** OR **Question B**. Each carries 7 marks per sub-part.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Retest Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a hash table with separate chaining. Construct a hash table of size 13 using the hash function $h(k) = (3k + 7) \bmod 13$ for the keys: 12, 25, 38, 51, 64, 7, 19, 32. Show the final hash table structure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Compute $h(k) = (3k + 7) \bmod 13$ for each key:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h(12) &= (36 + 7) \bmod 13 = 43 \bmod 13 = 4 \\
h(25) &= (75 + 7) \bmod 13 = 82 \bmod 13 = 4 \\
h(38) &= (114 + 7) \bmod 13 = 121 \bmod 13 = 4 \\
h(51) &= (153 + 7) \bmod 13 = 160 \bmod 13 = 4 \\
h(64) &= (192 + 7) \bmod 13 = 199 \bmod 13 = 4 \\
h(7)  &= (21 + 7) \bmod 13 = 28 \bmod 13 = 2 \\
h(19) &= (57 + 7) \bmod 13 = 64 \bmod 13 = 12 \\
h(32) &= (96 + 7) \bmod 13 = 103 \bmod 13 = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

Final Hash Table (Separate Chaining):

| Bucket | Chain |
|--------|-------|
| 0 | empty |
| 1 | empty |
| 2 | 7 → NULL |
| 3 | empty |
| 4 | 12 → 25 → 38 → 51 → 64 → NULL |
| 5 | empty |
| 6 | empty |
| 7 | empty |
| 8 | empty |
| 9 | empty |
| 10 | empty |
| 11 | empty |
| 12 | 19 → 32 → NULL |

Observations: All numeric keys from 12, 25, 38, 51, 64 collide into bucket 4 → heavy clustering. This is a **poor hash function** for this dataset. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 3 marks for computation, 2 marks for table, 1 mark for observation.)*

**(b)** Design and implement (algorithm + Python code) a spell checker using a hash table. Your implementation should:
- Load a dictionary from a file
- Validate individual words
- Suggest corrections for misspelled words using edit distance 1

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**

```
Algorithm SpellChecker(DictFile, InputWord)
1. Create HashTable HT of size M
2. For each word w in DictFile:
       HT.insert(w)
3. If HT.lookup(InputWord) is TRUE:
       return "CORRECT"
4. Else:
       candidates = GenerateEditDistance1(InputWord)
       suggestions = []
       For each c in candidates:
            If HT.lookup(c) is TRUE:
                suggestions.append(c)
       Return "MISSPELLED, suggestions = " + suggestions
```

**Edit-Distance-1 Generation:** For a word of length $L$:
- Deletions: $L$ variants
- Transpositions: $L - 1$ variants
- Replacements: $L \times 26$ variants
- Insertions: $(L+1) \times 26$ variants
- **Total: $54L + 25$ candidates**

**Python Code:** Refer to the complete implementation in **Section 3.4** of these notes. The `SpellChecker` class encapsulates:
- `HashTable` (lines 1–25) — chaining-based dictionary storage.
- `check_word()` (line 41) — O(1) validation.
- `edit_distance_1()` (lines 45–66) — candidate generation.
- `suggest()` (lines 68–70) — suggestion retrieval.

**Time Complexity:**
- Insertion: $O(L)$ per word, $O(DL)$ for the whole dictionary.
- Lookup: $O(L)$ per query.
- Suggestion: $O(54L^2 \cdot L) = O(L^3)$ per misspelled word.

*(Award 2 marks for algorithm, 3 marks for code, 1 mark for analysis, 1 mark for correctness/edge cases.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Regular Series]**

**(a)** What is the Levenshtein edit distance? Compute the edit distance between the words `"kitten"` and `"sitting"` using dynamic programming. Show the full DP table. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The **Levenshtein Edit Distance** between two strings is the minimum number of single-character insertions, deletions, or substitutions required to transform one string into the other.

**DP Table for `"kitten"` → `"sitting"`:**

|       | "" | s | i | t | t | i | n | g |
|-------|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|
| **""**| 0  | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| **k** | 1  | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| **i** | 2  | 2 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
| **t** | 3  | 3 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| **t** | 4  | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| **e** | 5  | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| **n** | 6  | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 3 |

**Final answer: $D[6][7] = 3$**

**Transformation path:**
- `kitten` → `sitten` (substitute `k` with `s`)
- `sitten` → `sittin` (delete `e`)
- `sittin` → `sitting` (insert `g`)

**Recurrence used at each cell:**

$$D[i][j] = 1 + \min\{D[i-1][j],\, D[i][j-1],\, D[i-1][j-1]\} \text{ if } A[i] \neq B[j]$$
$$D[i][j] = D[i-1][j-1] \text{ if } A[i] = B[j]$$

**Time Complexity:** $O(n \cdot m)$ where $n, m$ are the string lengths.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 4 marks for table, 1 mark for path, 1 mark for complexity.)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a hash-table based spell checker. Discuss why hashing is preferred over a Binary Search Tree (BST) for this use case. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Architecture Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the full flowchart).**

The hash-based spell checker consists of four modules:
1. **Dictionary Loader** — reads words and inserts them into the hash table.
2. **Hash Engine** — computes $h(w)$ for any word $w$ using a polynomial hash.
3. **Validator** — checks membership in $O(1)$ average time.
4. **Suggestion Engine** — generates ED-1 candidates and re-queries the hash table.

**Hashing vs BST for Spell Checking:**

| Parameter | Hash Table | BST |
|-----------|-----------|-----|
| Average Lookup | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Worst Lookup | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ (unbalanced) |
| Insertion | $O(1)$ avg | $O(\log n)$ |
| Ordered Traversal | Not supported | In-order is natural |
| Memory Overhead | Low (array of lists) | Higher (node pointers) |
| Spell-check Fit | **Excellent** (point queries) | Adequate |

**Justification:** Spell checking is a **point query workload** — "Is this word in the dictionary?" — and does not require ordered traversal. Hence, the constant-time $O(1)$ lookup of hash tables makes them the **industry-preferred choice**. BSTs shine when range queries, prefix searches, or ordered iteration are needed (e.g., autocomplete using prefix trees).

*(Award 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for comparison table, 2 marks for justification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize case** — `lookup("Hello")` must equal `lookup("hello")`. Examiners deduct 1 mark for case-sensitive bugs.
> 2. **Ignoring empty dictionary file** — Always include a fallback mini-dictionary in code. Lose 0.5 mark if the program crashes.
> 3. **Wrong hash modulus** — Using $m = 1000$ (non-prime) causes clustering. Prefer prime $m$.
> 4. **Not handling punctuation** — Tokens like `"hello,"` and `"hello!"` must be stripped before lookup. Examiners check for `re.findall(r"[A-Za-z]+", text)`.
> 5. **Returning only ONE suggestion** — Even if 5 valid corrections exist, return all (or top 5). Examiners expect a *list*.
> 6. **Confusing edit distance types** — Levenshtein does NOT include transposition by default; Damerau–Levenshtein does. Be explicit in the viva.
> 7. **No complexity analysis in viva** — Examiners ALWAYS ask time complexity. Be ready with $O(L)$, $O(1+\alpha)$, and $O(54L^2)$ numbers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- ✔ **Hash Table** = array of buckets + hash function + collision resolution.
- ✔ **Polynomial hash** formula: $h = \left(\sum \text{ord}(c_i) \cdot p^i \right) \bmod m$.
- ✔ **Load factor** $\alpha = n / m$; keep $\alpha \leq 0.75$ for good performance.
- ✔ **Separate Chaining** = linked list per bucket (used in lab).
- ✔ **Open Addressing** = Linear / Quadratic / Double Hashing.
- ✔ **Average Lookup** = $O(1 + \alpha)$; **Worst** = $O(n)$.
- ✔ **Edit Distance 1** = $54L + 25$ candidates (deletion + transposition + replacement + insertion).
- ✔ **Levenshtein DP** = $D[i][j] = 1 + \min(D[i-1][j], D[i][j-1], D[i-1][j-1])$ on mismatch.
- ✔ Always **lowercase** words before hashing and lookup.
- ✔ Always **strip punctuation** using regex `r"[A-Za-z]+"` before validation.
- ✔ Use a **prime $m$** (101, 1009, 10007) to minimize clustering.
- ✔ **Dictionary file** should have one word per line; always include a fallback dictionary.
- ✔ Suggestion engine should return a **list** of candidates, not a single string.
- ✔ **Viva one-liner:** *"Hashing gives O(1) lookup which is essential for real-time spell checking in large documents."*
- ✔ **No collision-free hash exists** (Pigeonhole principle) — design for efficient resolution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of a basic memory allocator and garbage collector using doubly linked list
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL307 - DATA STRUCTURES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of a basic memory allocator and garbage collector using doubly linked list

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Allocator (Dynamic Memory Manager):** A subsystem, typically part of the OS runtime or a programming language's standard library, that hands out contiguous chunks of a program's heap to user processes (`malloc`/`new`) and reclaims them (`free`/`delete`) when no longer needed. A **garbage collector (GC)** is a higher-level, automatic form of memory management that identifies and frees blocks no longer reachable from any live reference (root set), removing the programmer's burden of explicit `free`.

In this KTU 2024 Scheme Data Structures Lab experiment, the **heap is simulated as a single contiguous array of fixed size**, and a **doubly linked list** acts as the bookkeeping structure. Each node in the list represents one **memory block** (free or allocated) and stores:

- `start` — beginning address (array index) of the block
- `size` — length of the block in words/bytes
- `is_free` — boolean flag (0 = allocated, 1 = free)
- `prev`, `next` — pointers to the neighbouring blocks

The allocator walks this list to satisfy allocation requests and coalesces adjacent free blocks on deallocation. The garbage collector runs a **Mark–Sweep** pass: it *marks* all blocks reachable from a simulated root set, then *sweeps* (frees) every unmarked block.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Parking Lot

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Mall Parking Lot**

Imagine a long, single-row parking lot in front of a mall:

| Component | Parking Lot Analogy | Memory System |
|---|---|---|
| Total spaces | Total parking slots | Heap (fixed array) |
| Car parked | Block handed to a program | Allocated block |
| Empty slot | Vacant parking space | Free block |
| Lot attendant | Searches for a free slot | **Allocator** |
| Security guard with CCTV | Checks which cars are still owned by visitors | **Garbage Collector** |
| Ticket issued | Record of "this slot is taken" | Allocation metadata |

- The **attendant (allocator)** does *not* know which cars are abandoned; he only manages *space*.
- The **security guard (garbage collector)** walks the lot, looks at the active ticket holders (the root set), and tows away (sweeps) cars whose owners have left the mall (unreachable blocks).
- The **doubly linked list** is the attendant's clipboard — it lets him walk **forward** to find the next slot and **backward** to merge two adjacent empty slots instantly (just like a waiter pushing two empty tables together).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse the *allocator* with the *garbage collector*. The allocator is **space management** (where to put new data); the GC is **liveness management** (what existing data is still in use). They are two independent subsystems that work on the **same** heap.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Heap:** Region of memory from which dynamic allocations are served.
- **Block / Chunk:** A contiguous slice of the heap, either free or in-use.
- **Header / Metadata:** Small bookkeeping record stored *just before* (or as part of) each block.
- **Free List:** Doubly linked list of free (or *all*) blocks used to drive allocation.
- **Internal Fragmentation:** Wasted space *inside* an allocated block (e.g., rounding up to alignment).
- **External Fragmentation:** Wasted space *between* allocated blocks; total free memory exists but is non-contiguous.
- **Coalescing:** Merging two or more adjacent free blocks into one larger free block.
- **Root Set:** A set of pointers known to be live (e.g., stack variables, global variables, CPU registers).
- **Reachability:** A block is *live* if some path of pointers from the root set can reach it.
- **Mark–Sweep:** Two-phase GC: (1) mark reachable blocks, (2) sweep (free) unmarked blocks.
- **Reference Counting:** Per-block counter of incoming pointers; freed when count drops to 0.
- **Stop-the-World (STW):** GC phase during which the mutator (user program) is paused.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PCCSL307):** The lab expects students to (a) implement `mymalloc(n)` and `myfree(ptr)` on a simulated heap, (b) support **block splitting** on allocation and **boundary-tag coalescing** on free, and (c) implement a simple **mark–sweep garbage collector** driven by a user-supplied root set.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Simulated Memory Block

A block's metadata is stored in the doubly linked list node, **not inside the simulated array itself** (this keeps the simulation pure). Each node has the following layout:

$$
\text{Node} = \{ \text{start},\ \text{size},\ \text{is\_free},\ \text{prev},\ \text{next},\ \text{id} \}
$$

The simulated heap is a plain integer/char array `heap[0..H-1]`. The *list* of nodes is a separate structure that records the location and status of every region inside the array.

```
+-------------------+-------------------+-------------------+
|  Block 0 (used)   |  Block 1 (free)   |  Block 2 (used)   |
|  start=0 size=5   |  start=5 size=8   |  start=13 size=4  |
+-------------------+-------------------+-------------------+
        ↑                   ↑                   ↑
       head              (free)              tail
```

### 2.2 Allocation Strategies — The Three Classic Policies

Given a request of size $n$, the allocator scans the free list and chooses a block using one of three policies:

| Policy | Selection Rule | Strength | Weakness | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **First Fit** | First free block with $\text{size} \ge n$ | Fast (stops at first hit) | May leave small unusable fragments near the head | General-purpose `malloc` |
| **Best Fit** | Free block with smallest $\text{size} \ge n$ | Minimises wasted space per request | Slowest (full scan); causes *tiny* fragments | Memory-constrained embedded |
| **Worst Fit** | Free block with **largest** size | Leaves the remainder still large & usable | Worst large-block fragmentation | Rarely used in practice |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Cue:** When asked to compare policies, always quantify trade-off using:
> $$\text{Time}_{\text{FirstFit}} \le \text{Time}_{\text{BestFit}} \le \text{Time}_{\text{WorstFit}}$$
> $$\text{ExternalFragmentation}_{\text{WorstFit}} \ge \text{ExternalFragmentation}_{\text{BestFit}} \ge \text{ExternalFragmentation}_{\text{FirstFit}}$$

### 2.3 Block Splitting

After locating a free block of size $S$ where $S \ge n$, if the leftover $S - n$ is greater than some **minimum split size** $s_{\min}$ (so the leftover is itself usable), the block is **split** into two:

$$
\text{Allocated block:}\quad \text{size} = n,\quad \text{start} = \text{old\_start}
$$

$$
\text{Residual free block:}\quad \text{size} = S - n,\quad \text{start} = \text{old\_start} + n
$$

The residual is inserted into the free list adjacent to the allocated block. Splitting reduces *internal* waste but increases the *count* of nodes in the list.

### 2.4 Boundary-Tag Coalescing

When a block is freed, it is **merged** with any immediate free neighbour(s) using the `prev` and `next` pointers:

- **Case A — no neighbour free:** Just mark the block `is_free = 1`.
- **Case B — only `next` is free:** Merge with next; update sizes and links.
- **Case C — only `prev` is free:** Merge into prev; update sizes and links.
- **Case D — both neighbours free:** Merge all three (prev + current + next) into one.

The merged size is:

$$
\text{size}_{\text{merged}} = \text{size}_{\text{prev}} + \text{size}_{\text{current}} + \text{size}_{\text{next}}
$$

Coalescing is what makes a doubly linked list **mandatory** here — the `prev` pointer allows $O(1)$ left-merge without re-scanning the list.

### 2.5 KTU Formula & Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Total heap | $H$ | words/bytes (constant) |
| Used memory | $U = \sum_{i : \neg\text{free}(i)} \text{size}_i$ | bytes |
| Free memory | $F = H - U$ | bytes |
| External fragmentation | $\xi = 1 - \dfrac{\max(\text{free\_sizes})}{F}$ | unitless, $0 \le \xi < 1$ |
| Allocation time (First Fit) | $T_{\text{FF}} = O(N_{\text{free}})$ | $N_{\text{free}}$ = number of free blocks |
| Split condition | $S - n > s_{\min}$ | $s_{\min}$ usually $4$–$16$ bytes |
| Coalesced size | $s_{\text{merged}} = s_{\text{prev}} + s_{\text{cur}} + s_{\text{next}}$ | bytes |
| GC reachability closure | $\text{Reach}(R) = R \cup \text{pointers}(\text{Reach}(R))$ | recursive fixed-point |
| Mark phase cost | $O(\vert\text{Reach}(R)\vert)$ | visits each live block once |
| Sweep phase cost | $O(\text{total blocks})$ | visits every block once |
| Mark–Sweep total | $O(\text{total blocks})$ | independent of freed amount |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why doubly linked list, not singly?** A singly linked list gives you only forward traversal — to find the *previous* block during left-coalescing you would need an $O(N)$ scan. The doubly linked list makes **bidirectional coalescing $O(1)$** per neighbour, which is the entire point of using it in this experiment.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Use of These Concepts |
|---|---|
| **OS Kernels** | `kmalloc`/`kfree` in Linux uses a slab + buddy system inspired by these policies. |
| **Embedded / RTOS** | `FreeRTOS heap_4.c` is a first-fit allocator. Static allocation policies matter when fragmentation cannot be tolerated. |
| **Compilers / Runtimes** | Java HotSpot, Go runtime, Python (CPython) all use mark–sweep variants or tri-colour concurrent GC. |
| **Databases** | Buffer-pool managers use first-fit / best-fit to allocate fixed-size pages. |
| **Game Engines** | Custom pool allocators avoid GC pauses for real-time frame budgets. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Coalescing Decision Logic

Let $B_c$ be the block being freed, with neighbour $B_p$ (`prev`) and $B_n$ (`next`). Each block carries a flag $f_i \in \{0, 1\}$ where $1$ means *free*.

The merged size $M$ and the surviving block $B^*$ are:

$$
M = f_p \cdot s_p + s_c + f_n \cdot s_n
$$

$$
B^* = 
\begin{cases}
B_c & \text{if } f_p = 0 \text{ and } f_n = 0 \\
B_n & \text{if } f_p = 0 \text{ and } f_n = 1 \\
B_p & \text{if } f_p = 1 \text{ and } f_n = 0 \\
B_p & \text{if } f_p = 1 \text{ and } f_n = 1
\end{cases}
$$

**Derivation step 1** — In Case D (both free), we must keep *one* merged node, not three. Choosing $B_p$ as the survivor is the canonical choice because it is the leftmost; its `start` address is the lowest and so it remains a valid lower bound for the merged region.

**Derivation step 2** — The doubly linked list is re-stitched:

$$
B_p.\text{next} = B_n.\text{next}
$$

$$
B_n.\text{next}.\text{prev} = B_p \quad \text{(if } B_n.\text{next} \neq \text{NULL)}
$$

$$
B_p.\text{size} = s_p + s_c + s_n
$$

$$
B_p.\text{is\_free} = 1
$$

All three cases (A, B, C, D) are specialisations of the same algebraic identity; the only difference is which of $s_p$ or $s_n$ is added.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Allocation Algorithm (First-Fit with Splitting)

Let the free list be $L = [b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{k-1}]$ in order, with $f_i = 1$ for all $i$ (a free list of *only* free blocks; or we scan the *all-blocks* list filtering on $f_i = 1$). The request is $(n, a)$ where $n$ is the size and $a$ is a desired alignment.

**Step 1 — Linear scan:**

$$
i^\star = \min \{ i \in [0, k-1] : f_i = 1 \text{ and } s_i \ge n \}
$$

If no such $i$ exists, allocation fails (return `NULL` or raise `OOM`).

**Step 2 — Alignment check:** The returned start address must satisfy

$$
\text{start} \bmod a = 0
$$

If $b_{i^\star}.\text{start}$ does not satisfy this, we either slide the start forward (creating a tiny padding block) or fall back to the next candidate. The minimum block size after alignment is:

$$
n' = n + (a - (\text{start} \bmod a)) \bmod a
$$

**Step 3 — Split decision:**

$$
\text{split} = (s_{i^\star} - n') > s_{\min}
$$

If true, perform a split as in §2.3.

**Step 4 — Update the chosen node:**

$$
b_{i^\star}.\text{size} = n',\quad b_{i^\star}.\text{is\_free} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Return a handle** — typically the *index* of the block, which acts as the pointer the user later passes to `myfree`. This is why the simulation is convenient: pointers are just array indices.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Mark–Sweep Garbage Collection

Let $R = \{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_m\}$ be the **root set** — a list of indices the user declares as "definitely live". Each block $b$ contains a set of *outgoing* references $\text{ref}(b) \subseteq \{0, 1, \dots, k-1\}$.

**Step 1 — Mark phase (fixed-point traversal):**

Define a colour function $C : \{0,\dots,k-1\} \to \{\text{WHITE}, \text{GREY}, \text{BLACK}\}$.

1. Initialise: $C(b) = \text{WHITE}$ for all $b$.
2. For each $r \in R$: $C(r) = \text{GREY}$; push onto worklist $W$.
3. While $W \ne \emptyset$:
   - Pop $b$ from $W$.
   - $C(b) = \text{BLACK}$.
   - For each $b' \in \text{ref}(b)$:
     - If $C(b') = \text{WHITE}$, set $C(b') = \text{GREY}$; push $b'$ onto $W$.

This is the standard **tri-colour invariant** GC; it terminates because each block is pushed at most once.

**Step 2 — Sweep phase (linear pass):**

$$
\forall\, b : C(b) = \text{WHITE} \;\Longrightarrow\; b.\text{is\_free} = 1
$$

Then reset colours (or, equivalently, re-mark all free blocks WHITE for the next cycle):

$$
\forall\, b : C(b) = \text{WHITE}
$$

**Cost analysis:**

$$
T_{\text{mark}} = O\bigl(\vert\text{Reach}(R)\vert\bigr)
$$

$$
T_{\text{sweep}} = O(k)
$$

$$
T_{\text{GC}} = O(k) \quad \text{(amortised, ignoring the cost of finding references)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the doubly linked list again?** During the sweep, we walk the list *linearly* to free every WHITE block. The doubly linked structure means we can unlink a swept block in $O(1)$ by patching its `prev` and `next` — no second scan required.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Given:** Heap size $H = 64$ bytes. Free list initially has **one** free block: start $= 0$, size $= 64$, is\_free $= 1$. Minimum split size $s_{\min} = 4$. First-fit policy. Roots for GC: $R = \{0\}$.

**Operations:**

1. `mymalloc(20)` → scans, finds block $[0, 64]$, splits because $64 - 20 = 44 > 4$.
   - Allocated: start $= 0$, size $= 20$, is\_free $= 0$.
   - Residual free: start $= 20$, size $= 44$, is\_free $= 1$.

2. `mymalloc(15)` → scans, finds $[20, 44]$, splits because $44 - 15 = 29 > 4$.
   - Allocated: start $= 20$, size $= 15$, is\_free $= 0$.
   - Residual free: start $= 35$, size $= 29$, is\_free $= 1$.

3. `myfree(pointer to block @ start=20)` → coalesce with neighbours. `prev` (block @ 0) is allocated; `next` (block @ 35) is free. **Case C applies.**
   - Merge into `next`: new block start $= 20$, size $= 15 + 29 = 44$, is\_free $= 1$.
   - Old `prev` (block @ 0) now has its `next` pointer updated to this merged block.

4. **Final state:**

$$
\text{Block}_0: \text{start}=0,\ \text{size}=20,\ \text{is\_free}=0
$$

$$
\text{Block}_1: \text{start}=20,\ \text{size}=44,\ \text{is\_free}=1
$$

5. **Run GC with $R = \{0\}$:** Mark block 0 (BLACK). Block 1 stays WHITE → swept → stays free (no change). Sweep finishes; $0$ live blocks besides the root.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** A frequent exam question is *"After these allocations and one free, what does the list look like?"* Practice writing the list state explicitly after **every** operation; partial credit is given for each correctly drawn node.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Operation Cost Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Time Complexity | Why |
|---|---|---|
| `mymalloc(n)` — First Fit | $O(N)$ where $N$ = number of blocks in the all-blocks list | Linear scan to find first fit |
| `mymalloc(n)` — Best Fit | $O(N)$ | Full scan to find minimum-fit |
| `mymalloc(n)` — Worst Fit | $O(N)$ | Full scan to find max-fit |
| Split | $O(1)$ | Local re-stitching |
| `myfree(p)` | $O(1)$ | Constant-time neighbour lookup via `prev`/`next` |
| Coalesce (all 4 cases) | $O(1)$ | Local pointer updates |
| GC Mark | $O(\vert\text{Reach}(R)\vert)$ | Each live block visited once |
| GC Sweep | $O(N)$ | One pass over all blocks |
| Total GC cycle | $O(N)$ | Linear in heap bookkeeping size |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps how the user program, allocator, and garbage collector interact through the doubly linked list of block descriptors that wraps a flat simulated heap.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UserProg["User Program calls mymalloc n and myfree p"]
    Roots["Root Set R declared by user"]
    Alloc["Allocator: First Fit / Best Fit / Worst Fit"]
    Splitter["Block Splitter: if remainder greater than s_min, split"]
    FreeOps["myfree: mark is_free = 1"]
    Coalescer["Coalescer: merge prev or next or both free blocks"]
    Marker["GC Marker: tri colour traversal from R"]
    Sweeper["GC Sweeper: free every WHITE block"]
    HeapArray["Simulated Heap: char or int array size H"]
    DLL["Doubly Linked List of Block Descriptors"]
    OutResult["Return pointer index to user or NULL on OOM"]

    UserProg --> Alloc
    UserProg --> FreeOps
    UserProg --> Roots
    Roots --> Marker
    Alloc --> Splitter
    Splitter --> DLL
    Splitter --> HeapArray
    FreeOps --> Coalescer
    Coalescer --> DLL
    Coalescer --> HeapArray
    Marker --> DLL
    Sweeper --> DLL
    Sweeper --> HeapArray
    DLL --> OutResult
    HeapArray --> DLL
```

### 4.2 Doubly Linked List of Block Descriptors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef used fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#aa0000,color:#000
    classDef free fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef nullBox fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#666666,color:#000

    N0["B0 start 0 size 20 USED"]:::used
    N1["B1 start 20 size 10 FREE"]:::free
    N2["B2 start 30 size 15 USED"]:::used
    N3["B3 start 45 size 19 FREE"]:::free
    NNULL["NULL"]:::nullBox
    HEAD["HEAD"]:::nullBox
    TAIL["TAIL"]:::nullBox

    HEAD --> N0
    N0 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> NNULL

    N0 -.prev.-> HEAD
    N1 -.prev.-> N0
    N2 -.prev.-> N1
    N3 -.prev.-> N2
    N3 -.next tail.-> TAIL
```

### 4.3 mymalloc Decision Flow (First Fit with Splitting)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start["mymalloc n called"]
    Cur["cur = head of all blocks list"]
    LoopCheck{"cur is not NULL?"}
    FreeCheck{"cur.is_free = 1 AND cur.size greater or equal n?"}
    RemCheck{"cur.size minus n greater than s_min?"}
    Split["Split: allocated block size n, residual size cur.size minus n inserted as new free block"]
    MarkUsed["cur.is_free = 0, cur.size = n"]
    Advance["cur = cur.next"]
    ReturnPtr["Return cur.start as pointer to user"]
    Fail["Return NULL: out of memory"]

    Start --> Cur
    Cur --> LoopCheck
    LoopCheck -- yes --> FreeCheck
    LoopCheck -- no --> Fail
    FreeCheck -- yes --> RemCheck
    FreeCheck -- no --> Advance
    RemCheck -- yes --> Split
    RemCheck -- no --> MarkUsed
    Split --> ReturnPtr
    MarkUsed --> ReturnPtr
    Advance --> LoopCheck
```

### 4.4 myfree and Coalescing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FreeStart["myfree p called: locate block cur by p"]
    Mark["cur.is_free = 1"]
    PrevFree{"prev exists AND prev.is_free = 1?"}
    NextFree{"next exists AND next.is_free = 1?"}
    CaseA["Case A: no neighbour free, done"]
    CaseB["Case B: only next is free, merge cur into next, fix prev of next.next, fix next of prev"]
    CaseC["Case C: only prev is free, merge cur into prev, fix prev of next, fix next of prev.prev"]
    CaseD["Case D: both prev and next free, merge all into prev, fix next of next.next, drop cur and next from list"]
    EndFree["Coalescing complete"]

    FreeStart --> Mark
    Mark --> PrevFree
    PrevFree -- no --> NextFree
    PrevFree -- yes --> NextFree
    NextFree -- no --> CaseC
    NextFree -- yes --> CaseD
    PrevFree -- no --> NextFree
    NextFree -- no --> CaseA
    NextFree -- yes --> CaseB
    CaseA --> EndFree
    CaseB --> EndFree
    CaseC --> EndFree
    CaseD --> EndFree
```

### 4.5 Mark Sweep Garbage Collector

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GCStart["GC trigger: manual or auto after N mallocs"]
    Init["For every block b: colour b = WHITE, clear visited flag"]
    PushRoots["For every r in root set R: colour r = GREY, push to worklist W"]
    WEmpty{"W empty?"}
    Pop["Pop block b from W"]
    MarkBlack["colour b = BLACK"]
    Refs{"b has outgoing references ref b?"}
    PushRef["For every child c in ref b: if colour c = WHITE, colour c = GREY and push to W"]
    SweepStart["Sweep pass over entire list"]
    IsWhite{"colour b = WHITE?"}
    FreeIt["b.is_free = 1, reset colour to WHITE"]
    KeepIt["Reset colour to WHITE, leave is_free unchanged"]
    SweepEnd["GC done, return blocks freed count"]

    GCStart --> Init
    Init --> PushRoots
    PushRoots --> WEmpty
    WEmpty -- no --> Pop
    Pop --> MarkBlack
    MarkBlack --> Refs
    Refs -- yes --> PushRef
    Refs -- no --> WEmpty
    PushRef --> WEmpty
    WEmpty -- yes --> SweepStart
    SweepStart --> IsWhite
    IsWhite -- yes --> FreeIt
    IsWhite -- no --> KeepIt
    FreeIt --> SweepEnd
    KeepIt --> SweepEnd
```

### 4.6 Memory Layout After a Sequence of Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef used fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#aa0000,color:#000
    classDef free fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600,color:#000

    M0["addr 0 to 19 size 20 USED"]:::used
    M1["addr 20 to 29 size 10 FREE"]:::free
    M2["addr 30 to 44 size 15 USED"]:::used
    M3["addr 45 to 63 size 19 FREE"]:::free

    M0 --- M1 --- M2 --- M3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The colours red = allocated (in-use) and green = free are a **universal** convention in memory-visualisation tools (e.g., Valgrind's `massif`, jemalloc's stats). Adopt the same convention in your lab record.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCCSL307]**

**Q1. Differentiate between internal and external fragmentation. Which type is dominant in a first-fit allocator, and how does coalescing help?**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Internal fragmentation (1 mark):** Wasted space *inside* an allocated block, caused by rounding up the size to alignment or split-minimum boundaries. The user is given more memory than requested.
> - **External fragmentation (1 mark):** Wasted space *between* allocated blocks — free memory exists in total, but in non-contiguous small chunks that cannot satisfy a larger request.
> - **First-fit & coalescing (1 mark):** First-fit tends to create many small free holes near the head of the list, so **external fragmentation dominates**. Coalescing merges adjacent free blocks back into larger contiguous free regions, reducing external fragmentation and improving the chance that a future large allocation succeeds.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCCSL307]**

**Q2. What is the role of a doubly linked list in a memory allocator? Why is a singly linked list insufficient?**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - The doubly linked list is the **bookkeeping structure** that records every block's start, size, free/used flag, and neighbours (1 mark).
> - When a block is freed, the allocator must check the `prev` and `next` blocks to decide whether to **coalesce**. With a doubly linked list, both neighbours are reachable in $O(1)$ (1 mark).
> - A singly linked list can reach only the `next` block in $O(1)$; to reach the `prev` block you would have to scan from the head, making left-coalescing $O(N)$ and degrading the deallocation time complexity (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCCSL307 Module Experiment 2]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Memory Allocator Design*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Design a simulated memory allocator using a **doubly linked list of block descriptors** wrapping a flat heap array of size $H = 100$. Implement `mymalloc(int n)` using the **First-Fit** policy with block splitting (minimum split size $s_{\min} = 4$). Clearly state the node structure and write the algorithm in pseudocode.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Implement `myfree(int ptr)` with **boundary-tag coalescing** covering all four cases (A, B, C, D). Show with a worked example how a list evolves through the sequence: `mymalloc(20)`, `mymalloc(15)`, `mymalloc(25)`, `myfree(start=20)`, `myfree(start=35)`.

---

##### Model Solution

**(a) Node structure and `mymalloc` algorithm (7 marks):**

**Node structure (2 marks):**

$$
\text{struct Block} \{ \text{int start};\ \text{int size};\ \text{int is\_free};\ \text{Block}^\ast\ \text{prev};\ \text{Block}^\ast\ \text{next};\ \text{int id};\ \}
$$

- `start` — index in `heap[]` where block begins
- `size` — length in words
- `is_free` — `1` if free, `0` if allocated
- `prev`, `next` — doubly linked list pointers
- `id` — unique block identifier (for debugging)

**`mymalloc(int n)` — First-Fit with splitting (5 marks):**

```
function mymalloc(n):
    cur = head
    while cur is not NULL:
        if cur.is_free == 1 and cur.size >= n:
            if cur.size - n > s_min:
                # Split: create a new free block for the residual
                new_node = allocate_node()
                new_node.start = cur.start + n
                new_node.size  = cur.size - n
                new_node.is_free = 1
                # Splice new_node between cur and cur.next
                new_node.prev = cur
                new_node.next = cur.next
                if cur.next is not NULL:
                    cur.next.prev = new_node
                cur.next = new_node
                cur.size = n
            # Mark the chosen block as used
            cur.is_free = 0
            return cur.start      # simulated pointer
        cur = cur.next
    return -1                     # out of memory
```

**Mark distribution (a):** node structure 2 m, scan loop 1 m, split logic 1 m, return value 0.5 m, edge cases (head/tail) 0.5 m.

---

**(b) `myfree` with coalescing and worked example (7 marks):**

**`myfree(int ptr)` algorithm (3 marks):**

```
function myfree(ptr):
    cur = find_block_by_start(ptr)
    if cur is NULL or cur.is_free == 1:
        return error
    cur.is_free = 1

    # Try to merge with next
    if cur.next is not NULL and cur.next.is_free == 1:
        next = cur.next
        cur.size = cur.size + next.size
        cur.next = next.next
        if next.next is not NULL:
            next.next.prev = cur
        free_node(next)

    # Try to merge with prev
    if cur.prev is not NULL and cur.prev.is_free == 1:
        prev = cur.prev
        prev.size = prev.size + cur.size
        prev.next = cur.next
        if cur.next is not NULL:
            cur.next.prev = prev
        free_node(cur)
```

The four cases are handled automatically:
- **A:** Both `if` conditions false — no merge.
- **B:** Only the `next`-merge branch fires.
- **C:** Only the `prev`-merge branch fires.
- **D:** Both branches fire in sequence.

**Worked example (4 marks):**

Initial state: `H = 100`, one free block `[0, 100]`.

| Step | Operation | Resulting blocks (`start / size / free?`) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `mymalloc(20)` | `[0/20/0]`, `[20/80/1]` |
| 2 | `mymalloc(15)` | `[0/20/0]`, `[20/15/0]`, `[35/65/1]` |
| 3 | `mymalloc(25)` | `[0/20/0]`, `[20/15/0]`, `[35/25/0]`, `[60/40/1]` |
| 4 | `myfree(start=20)` | `prev [0/20/0]` used, `next [35/25/0]` used → **Case A**, no merge. Result: `[0/20/0]`, `[20/15/1]`, `[35/25/0]`, `[60/40/1]` |
| 5 | `myfree(start=35)` | `prev [20/15/1]` free, `next [60/40/1]` free → **Case D**, merge all into `[20/15+25+40] = [20/80/1]`. Result: `[0/20/0]`, `[20/80/1]` |

**Mark distribution (b):** algorithm 2 m, case mapping 1 m, table 1 m (1 mark per 2 correct rows, 2 marks for the post-merge final state).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Garbage Collector Design*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **Mark–Sweep garbage collection algorithm** in the context of this experiment. Define the *root set* and *reachability*. Write the pseudocode for both phases and analyse the time complexity.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Given a heap of size $H = 50$ with five blocks, where the root set is $R = \{0, 1\}$ and the reference relation is `ref(0) = {2, 3}`, `ref(1) = {}`, `ref(2) = {4}`, `ref(3) = {1}`, `ref(4) = {}`. Simulate the **Mark phase** step-by-step using the tri-colour abstraction, and state which blocks the **Sweep phase** will free.

---

##### Model Solution

**(a) Mark–Sweep explanation and pseudocode (7 marks):**

- **Root set (1 mark):** The set of pointers that the runtime *knows* are live — typically stack variables, global variables, and CPU registers. In the simulation, the user supplies a list of block IDs that act as roots.
- **Reachability (1 mark):** A block is *reachable* if there exists a path of pointers from some root to it. The set of reachable blocks is the smallest fixed point $L = R \cup \text{pointers}(L)$.
- **Mark phase pseudocode (2 marks):**
  ```
  for each block b: colour(b) = WHITE
  for each r in R: colour(r) = GREY; push r onto worklist W
  while W is not empty:
      b = pop(W)
      colour(b) = BLACK
      for each c in ref(b):
          if colour(c) == WHITE:
              colour(c) = GREY
              push c onto W
  ```
- **Sweep phase pseudocode (1 mark):**
  ```
  for each block b in heap order:
      if colour(b) == WHITE:
          b.is_free = 1            # unreachable, reclaim
      colour(b) = WHITE            # reset for next cycle
  ```
- **Complexity (1 mark):** Mark is $O(\vert\text{Reach}(R)\vert)$ because each block is pushed at most once. Sweep is $O(k)$ over $k$ total blocks. Total cycle is $O(k)$.
- **Trade-off note (1 mark):** Mark–sweep is *non-moving* — it never relocates live blocks — so it preserves pointers and is simple, but it suffers from fragmentation and a long *stop-the-world* pause proportional to heap size.

---

**(b) Worked Mark–Sweep simulation (7 marks):**

**Initial colours:** all WHITE. Worklist $W = \emptyset$. Roots $R = \{0, 1\}$.

| Step | Action | $W$ after action | Colours after action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mark roots: colour(0)=GREY, colour(1)=GREY | $[0, 1]$ | W W G G W W W (others WHITE) |
| 2 | Pop 0; colour(0)=BLACK; visit ref(0)={2,3}: 2 is WHITE → push, colour(2)=GREY; 3 is WHITE → push, colour(3)=GREY | $[1, 2, 3]$ | B W G G W W W |
| 3 | Pop 1; colour(1)=BLACK; ref(1)={} | $[2, 3]$ | B B G G W W W |
| 4 | Pop 2; colour(2)=BLACK; visit ref(2)={4}: 4 is WHITE → push, colour(4)=GREY | $[3, 4]$ | B B B G G W W |
| 5 | Pop 3; colour(3)=BLACK; visit ref(3)={1}: 1 is already BLACK, skip | $[4]$ | B B B B G W W |
| 6 | Pop 4; colour(4)=BLACK; ref(4)={} | $[]$ | B B B B B W W |
| 7 | $W$ empty → mark phase ends | — | — |

**Result:** Blocks 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 are **BLACK** (live). Blocks 5, 6 are **WHITE** (unreachable).

**Sweep phase:** Blocks 5 and 6 are reclaimed — set `is_free = 1`.

**Mark distribution (b):** step-by-step table 4 m (1 m for each of steps 1, 2, 4, 6 — the most discriminating rows); final answer 2 m; complexity recap 1 m.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in PCCSL307 Lab Exam**
>
> 1. **Confusing allocator with garbage collector (–1 to –2 marks):** The allocator handles *space* (first-fit, best-fit, splitting); the GC handles *liveness* (mark, sweep). Examiners deduct marks for blending the two.
> 2. **Forgetting to coalesce the *previous* block (–2 marks):** The most common bug in `myfree`. Without left-coalescing, external fragmentation explodes. Always check `cur.prev.is_free` *after* the right-coalesce, not before.
> 3. **Not updating `prev` pointers during a split (–1 mark):** When you splice a new free block between `cur` and `cur.next`, you must set `new_node.prev = cur` **and** `cur.next.prev = new_node`. Forgetting the latter leaves a dangling `prev` pointer.
> 4. **Returning the wrong "pointer" (–1 mark):** The simulation typically returns the `start` index (or block ID) — not a C `void*` address. Examiners look for explicit return type comments.
> 5. **In mark–sweep, forgetting to reset colours after sweep (–1 mark):** The next GC cycle will then mis-classify all BLACK blocks as live regardless of actual reachability, producing a "GC does nothing" bug.
> 6. **Omitting the `is_free` reset of coalesced blocks (–1 mark):** When you merge two free blocks, the *consumed* node's `is_free` is irrelevant because it is freed as a node — but the survivor's `is_free` must remain `1`. Examiners check this.
> 7. **Time complexity in words, not big-O (–1 mark):** Always state the complexity in **$O(\cdot)$ notation** and explain *why* (e.g., "$O(N)$ because we scan at most $N$ blocks").

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memory Allocator + GC with Doubly Linked List**

- [x] A **memory allocator** manages *space*; a **garbage collector** manages *liveness*. They share the heap but are independent subsystems.
- [x] The doubly linked list node carries: `start`, `size`, `is_free`, `prev`, `next` (and optionally `id`).
- [x] Three allocation policies: **First-Fit** (fast, more external fragmentation), **Best-Fit** (slow, smallest leftover), **Worst-Fit** (slow, largest leftover).
- [x] **Block splitting** happens iff `cur.size − n > s_min`; the residual is inserted as a new free node.
- [x] **Coalescing** has 4 cases — A (none), B (next), C (prev), D (both) — and runs in $O(1)$ thanks to `prev`/`next`.
- [x] Doubly linked list is **mandatory** for $O(1)$ left-coalescing; singly linked list forces $O(N)$ scan.
- [x] Garbage collection **Mark–Sweep** is a 2-phase algorithm: tri-colour mark, then linear sweep. WHITE at sweep time ⇒ free.
- [x] The **root set** $R$ is the user's declaration of "definitely live" pointers; without roots, *nothing* is reachable.
- [x] Mark cost is $O(\vert\text{Reach}(R)\vert)$; sweep cost is $O(\text{total blocks})$; total cycle is $O(\text{total blocks})$.
- [x] Always **reset colours** to WHITE at the end of sweep; otherwise the next GC cycle mis-behaves.
- [x] **Internal vs External fragmentation:** internal = inside a block (alignment, split min); external = between blocks (coalescing fixes this).
- [x] Return value of `mymalloc` is the block's `start` index, which acts as the simulated pointer for `myfree`.
- [x] Boundary-tag coalescing: when freeing block $b_c$, if `prev` or `next` is free, merge into the survivor (typically `prev` for left merge, `b_c` for right merge).
- [x] Mark–Sweep is **non-moving**: live blocks keep their addresses. This avoids updating pointers but causes fragmentation.
- [x] Real-world parallels: Linux `kmalloc` slab, FreeRTOS `heap_4.c` (first-fit), Java HotSpot (mark–sweep variants), CPython (reference counting + cycle collector).
- [x] Time complexity summary: `mymalloc` $O(N)$, `myfree` $O(1)$, GC cycle $O(N)$ — independent of how much memory is freed.
- [x] Lab deliverable expectations: working `mymalloc`/`myfree`, demonstration of splitting and coalescing, GC trigger and output, well-labelled diagrams in the record.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Find the maximum activity points that can be earned by participating in at most k events from an array of event points
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL307 - DATA STRUCTURES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Find the maximum activity points that can be earned by participating in at most k events from an array of event points

---

# 🎯 Finding Maximum Activity Points from K Events

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> Given an array `points[]` of size `n` where each element represents the points scored (which may be positive, zero, or negative) in a college event, the problem asks us to determine the **maximum total points** a student can earn by participating in **at most `k` events**. The selection is typically constrained to either any `k` elements (free selection) or `k` **consecutive** events (a sliding window).

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Standard KTU Variants of This Experiment:**
> 1. **Variant A (Free Selection):** Pick any `k` events from the array to maximize points.
> 2. **Variant B (Consecutive Selection):** Pick exactly `k` **consecutive** events to maximize points. This is the famous **"Maximum Sum Subarray of Size K"** problem.
>
> The KTU lab manual usually tests **Variant B** (consecutive events), as it elegantly combines **Prefix Sum** with **Sliding Window** — both core Module 2 topics.

### 🧠 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a college fest coordinator. You have **7 days** of events scheduled, and each event awards you points. You can attend **at most 3 consecutive days** of events. On which 3-day **streak** should you attend to score the maximum total points?

> [!TIP]
> Think of it as a **"hot streak"** problem. You want to find the best uninterrupted run of `k` days so that the sum of points earned across those days is maximum. You would never want to skip a high-point day inside your streak, so the **window must be contiguous**.

### Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| `points[]` | Input array of event points | `{2, 1, -5, 4, -1, 2, 3}` |
| `n` | Total number of events | `7` |
| `k` | Maximum events you can attend | `3` |
| `Prefix Sum` | Cumulative sum up to index `i` | `Prefix[3] = 2 + 1 + (-5) = -2` |
| **Window** | A contiguous subarray of size `k` | `{4, -1, 2}` → sum = `5` |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU board-exam trap: students often confuse "at most k" with "exactly k." If participating in fewer than `k` events gives a higher total (e.g., when negative values are present and skipping yields a better sum), the optimal answer might be from **less than k** events. Always read the problem carefully.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔬 Algorithmic Strategy: Prefix Sum + Sliding Window

The problem reduces to a simple question: **"Out of all contiguous subarrays of size k, which has the maximum sum?"**

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Read Input:** Accept the array `points[]` of size `n` and the integer `k`.
2. **Compute Prefix Sum Array:** Build `prefix[i] = points[0] + points[1] + ... + points[i-1]` (size `n+1`).
   - This is done in **O(n)** time using a single pass.
3. **Initial Window:** Compute the sum of the first `k` elements directly, OR use prefix sum: `currentSum = prefix[k] - prefix[0]`.
4. **Slide the Window:** For each starting index `i` from `1` to `n - k`:
   - Compute new window sum in **O(1)** using: `windowSum = prefix[i + k] - prefix[i]`
   - Update `maxSum = max(maxSum, windowSum)`
5. **Return** `maxSum`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Prefix Sum is the KTU-preferred approach?**
> A naïve O(n·k) double loop recomputes each window sum from scratch. The prefix-sum technique converts each window sum computation to **O(1)**, achieving overall **O(n)** time complexity. KTU examiners explicitly reward students who recognize this optimization.

### 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Unit / Domain | Time Cost |
|---------|-------------|---------------|-----------|
| $Prefix[i] = \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} points[j]$ | Cumulative sum (0-indexed prefix) | Integer | O(n) |
| $WindowSum(i) = Prefix[i+k] - Prefix[i]$ | Sum of window of size k starting at i | Integer | O(1) per window |
| $MaxSum = \max_{0 \le i \le n-k} \big( Prefix[i+k] - Prefix[i] \big)$ | Final answer | Integer | O(n) |
| $InitialWindow = \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} points[j]$ | Sum of first window (alternative) | Integer | O(k) |
| $TimeComplexity = O(n)$ | One pass for prefix + one pass for windows | — | Optimal |
| $SpaceComplexity = O(n)$ | Extra prefix array of size n+1 | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Free-Selection Variant Formula:** If you can pick **any** k events (not necessarily consecutive), the answer is:
> $$\text{MaxSum}_{\text{free}} = \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \text{points}_{\text{sorted-desc}}[i]$$
> This requires sorting (O(n log n)) or a min-heap of size k (O(n log k)).

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Stock Market Analysis:** Find the best `k`-day window of returns to compute rolling averages.
- **Signal Processing:** Identify the `k`-sample window with maximum energy in a digital signal.
- **Database Query Optimization:** Sliding window aggregates are used in SQL window functions.
- **Bioinformatics:** Find the most active `k`-base-pair window in a DNA sequence activity map.
- **Network Monitoring:** Detect the `k`-second interval with peak traffic in a router log.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📊 Worked Example (Consecutive Selection)

### Input

$$
\text{points} = [2, 1, -5, 4, -1, 2, 3], \quad n = 7, \quad k = 3
$$

### Step 1 — Build the Prefix Sum Array

$$
\begin{aligned}
Prefix[0] &= 0 \\
Prefix[1] &= 0 + 2 = 2 \\
Prefix[2] &= 2 + 1 = 3 \\
Prefix[3] &= 3 + (-5) = -2 \\
Prefix[4] &= -2 + 4 = 2 \\
Prefix[5] &= 2 + (-1) = 1 \\
Prefix[6] &= 1 + 2 = 3 \\
Prefix[7] &= 3 + 3 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

> 📝 **Logic:** Each `Prefix[i]` stores the cumulative sum of the **first i elements**. The leading `Prefix[0] = 0` is a sentinel that allows uniform window-sum formulas.

### Step 2 — Compute Initial Window (i = 0)

$$
\text{WindowSum}(0) = Prefix[0 + 3] - Prefix[0] = Prefix[3] - Prefix[0] = -2 - 0 = -2
$$

> 📝 **Logic:** Window covers indices `0, 1, 2` → `points = [2, 1, -5]` → sum = `-2`. ✓ Correct.

Initialize: `maxSum = -2`.

### Step 3 — Slide the Window for i = 1 to n - k = 4

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{WindowSum}(1) &= Prefix[1+3] - Prefix[1] = Prefix[4] - Prefix[1] = 2 - 2 = 0 \\
\text{WindowSum}(2) &= Prefix[2+3] - Prefix[2] = Prefix[5] - Prefix[2] = 1 - 3 = -2 \\
\text{WindowSum}(3) &= Prefix[3+3] - Prefix[3] = Prefix[6] - Prefix[3] = 3 - (-2) = 5 \\
\text{WindowSum}(4) &= Prefix[4+3] - Prefix[4] = Prefix[7] - Prefix[4] = 6 - 2 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Update Maximum

$$
\begin{aligned}
\maxSum &= \max(-2, 0, -2, 5, 4) \\
        &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

### ✅ Final Result

$$
\boxed{\text{Maximum Activity Points} = 5}
$$

> The optimal streak is `points[3..5] = [4, -1, 2]`, summing to `4 + (-1) + 2 = 5`. 🎉

## 🧮 Analytical Matrix (Full Trace Table)

| Window Start `i` | Window Elements | Direct Sum | Prefix Formula | WindowSum | Running maxSum |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | `[2, 1, -5]` | `-2` | $Prefix[3] - Prefix[0]$ | `-2` | `-2` |
| 1 | `[1, -5, 4]` | `0` | $Prefix[4] - Prefix[1]$ | `0` | `0` |
| 2 | `[-5, 4, -1]` | `-2` | $Prefix[5] - Prefix[2]$ | `-2` | `0` |
| 3 | `[4, -1, 2]` | `5` | $Prefix[6] - Prefix[3]$ | `5` | `5` ⭐ |
| 4 | `[-1, 2, 3]` | `4` | $Prefix[7] - Prefix[4]$ | `4` | `5` |

## 💻 Complete KTU-Style C Program

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int maxActivityPoints(int points[], int n, int k) {
    int prefix[100];   // Assuming n <= 100
    int i, maxSum, windowSum;

    // Step 1: Build prefix sum array
    prefix[0] = 0;
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        prefix[i + 1] = prefix[i] + points[i];
    }

    // Step 2: Compute initial window sum
    maxSum = prefix[k] - prefix[0];

    // Step 3: Slide the window
    for (i = 1; i <= n - k; i++) {
        windowSum = prefix[i + k] - prefix[i];
        if (windowSum > maxSum) {
            maxSum = windowSum;
        }
    }

    return maxSum;
}

int main(void) {
    int points[] = {2, 1, -5, 4, -1, 2, 3};
    int n = 7;
    int k = 3;
    int result;

    result = maxActivityPoints(points, n, k);

    printf("Input Array : ");
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        printf("%d ", points[i]);
    }
    printf("\nWindow Size : k = %d\n", k);
    printf("Maximum Activity Points = %d\n", result);

    return 0;
}
```

### 🖥️ Sample Output

```
Input Array : 2 1 -5 4 -1 2 3
Window Size : k = 3
Maximum Activity Points = 5
```

### Edge Cases Checklist

| Edge Case | Expected Behavior | Test Input |
|---|---|---|
| All negative values | Return the **least negative** (largest) window | `{-2,-3,-1}, k=2` → `-3` |
| `k = 1` | Return the maximum single element | `{5,2,7,1}, k=1` → `7` |
| `k = n` | Return sum of entire array | `{1,2,3}, k=3` → `6` |
| `k > n` | Print error, return `-1` (invalid) | `{1,2}, k=5` |
| Single element array | Return that element | `{42}, k=1` → `42` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Validation Pitfall:** KTU examiners frequently test the `k > n` case. Always add a guard clause at the start of the function: `if (k > n) { /* handle error */ }`.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** 
**Define a prefix sum array. How does it help in computing the sum of a contiguous subarray in O(1) time?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A prefix sum array `prefix[i]` stores the cumulative sum of the array elements from index `0` to `i-1`. Formally:
$$prefix[i] = \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} arr[j], \quad prefix[0] = 0$$
The sum of any contiguous subarray from index `L` to `R` (inclusive) can then be computed in **O(1)** time as:
$$\text{SubarraySum}(L, R) = prefix[R+1] - prefix[L]$$
This eliminates the need to traverse the subarray each time, reducing the total time complexity from O(n·k) to **O(n)** when finding maximum-sum windows of size k. **(3 marks — Full allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for complexity benefit.)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is the time and space complexity of the maximum activity points algorithm using prefix sums? Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Time Complexity: O(n)** — A single pass builds the prefix array in O(n), and a second pass checks all `(n-k+1)` windows in O(n).
- **Space Complexity: O(n)** — An additional prefix array of size `n+1` is required.
- **Justification:** Unlike the brute-force O(n·k) approach that re-sums each window, the prefix-sum technique reuses precomputed cumulative sums, making each window query O(1). **(3 marks — 1 for time, 1 for space, 1 for justification.)**

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 🎯 Question A (14 Marks) — Algorithm Design & Implementation Focus

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

#### **(a)** Design an algorithm to find the maximum sum of `k` consecutive elements in an array of size `n` using the **prefix sum technique**. Write the algorithm in **pseudocode** and explain its working with the array `points = {2, 1, -5, 4, -1, 2, 3}` and `k = 3`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**

```
ALGORITHM MaxActivityPoints(points[0..n-1], n, k)
BEGIN
    IF k > n THEN
        PRINT "Invalid: k cannot exceed n"
        RETURN -1
    END IF
    
    // Step 1: Build prefix sum array
    prefix[0] ← 0
    FOR i ← 0 TO n-1 DO
        prefix[i+1] ← prefix[i] + points[i]
    END FOR
    
    // Step 2: Initialize maxSum with first window
    maxSum ← prefix[k] - prefix[0]
    
    // Step 3: Slide window across the array
    FOR i ← 1 TO n-k DO
        windowSum ← prefix[i+k] - prefix[i]
        IF windowSum > maxSum THEN
            maxSum ← windowSum
        END IF
    END FOR
    
    RETURN maxSum
END
```

**Working Trace:**

| `i` | Window | $prefix[i+k] - prefix[i]$ | `maxSum` |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | `[2, 1, -5]` | $-2 - 0 = -2$ | `-2` |
| 1 | `[1, -5, 4]` | $2 - 2 = 0$ | `0` |
| 2 | `[-5, 4, -1]` | $1 - 3 = -2$ | `0` |
| 3 | `[4, -1, 2]` | $3 - (-2) = 5$ | `5` ⭐ |
| 4 | `[-1, 2, 3]` | $6 - 2 = 4$ | `5` |

**Output:** Maximum Activity Points = **5**, achieved at window starting index `3`. **(7 marks — 2 for pseudocode, 3 for trace table, 2 for correct output.)**

---

#### **(b)** Implement the above algorithm in **C language**. Also analyze its **time and space complexity** and state **two real-world applications** of this technique. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (C Program):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int maxActivityPoints(int points[], int n, int k) {
    int prefix[100], i, maxSum, windowSum;
    
    if (k > n || k <= 0) return -1;
    
    prefix[0] = 0;
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        prefix[i + 1] = prefix[i] + points[i];
    }
    
    maxSum = prefix[k];
    for (i = 1; i <= n - k; i++) {
        windowSum = prefix[i + k] - prefix[i];
        if (windowSum > maxSum) maxSum = windowSum;
    }
    
    return maxSum;
}

int main() {
    int points[] = {2, 1, -5, 4, -1, 2, 3};
    int n = 7, k = 3;
    printf("Maximum Activity Points = %d\n", 
            maxActivityPoints(points, n, k));
    return 0;
}
```

**Complexity Analysis:**
- **Time Complexity: O(n)** — Single pass to build prefix + single pass for window sliding.
- **Space Complexity: O(n)** — Extra prefix array of size n+1.

**Two Real-World Applications:**
1. **Stock Market Analysis:** Finding the `k`-day window with maximum cumulative return for technical indicators like the Moving Average Convergence.
2. **Network Traffic Monitoring:** Detecting the `k`-second interval of peak bandwidth usage in router logs to identify bottlenecks. **(7 marks — 3 for correct code, 2 for complexity, 2 for applications.)**

---

### 🎯 Question B (14 Marks) — Variant Problem & Theoretical Depth

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]**

#### **(a)** Modify the above problem: Given an array of `n` event points where points can be **negative or positive**, find the maximum points attainable by attending **at most `k` non-consecutive events**. State the modified algorithm and explain why the prefix-sum technique is **not directly applicable** here. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Modified Algorithm (Sort + Greedy):**

```
ALGORITHM MaxPointsAtMostK(points[0..n-1], n, k)
BEGIN
    // Step 1: Sort array in descending order
    SORT points in descending order
    
    // Step 2: Greedily pick top events
    currentSum ← 0
    count ← 0
    
    FOR i ← 0 TO n-1 AND count < k DO
        IF points[i] > 0 THEN
            currentSum ← currentSum + points[i]
            count ← count + 1
        ELSE
            EXIT loop   // Remaining values are non-positive
        END IF
    END FOR
    
    RETURN currentSum
END
```

**Why Prefix Sum Fails Here:**
1. The "at most k non-consecutive" problem involves **arbitrary subset selection**, not contiguous windows. Prefix sums only capture contiguous ranges.
2. Adding a negative event would **decrease** the sum, but the constraint says "at most k," so we should **stop** before adding negatives.
3. The optimal answer may involve **fewer than k events** if adding more would only bring in negative values. Prefix sum cannot capture this trade-off.

**Trace Example:** For `points = {2, 1, -5, 4, -1, 2, 3}` and `k = 4`:
- Sorted descending: `{4, 3, 2, 2, 1, -1, -5}`
- Pick positives: `4 + 3 + 2 + 2 = 11` (only 4 events, matches k)
- Answer: **11** (Note: picking 5th positive `1` is allowed only if k ≥ 5; here it is not.)

**(7 marks — 2 for algorithm, 3 for explaining prefix-sum failure, 2 for trace.)**

---

#### **(b)** Implement the modified algorithm in **C**, and compare its **complexity** with the prefix-sum approach. Discuss when each approach is preferable. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (C Program):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

void sortDescending(int arr[], int n) {
    int i, j, temp;
    for (i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
        for (j = 0; j < n - i - 1; j++) {
            if (arr[j] < arr[j + 1]) {
                temp = arr[j];
                arr[j] = arr[j + 1];
                arr[j + 1] = temp;
            }
        }
    }
}

int maxPointsAtMostK(int points[], int n, int k) {
    int i, sum = 0, picked = 0;
    sortDescending(points, n);
    for (i = 0; i < n && picked < k; i++) {
        if (points[i] > 0) {
            sum += points[i];
            picked++;
        } else {
            break;
        }
    }
    return sum;
}

int main() {
    int points[] = {2, 1, -5, 4, -1, 2, 3};
    int n = 7, k = 4;
    printf("Maximum Points (at most k) = %d\n",
            maxPointsAtMostK(points, n, k));
    return 0;
}
```

**Complexity Comparison Table:**

| Approach | Time | Space | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prefix Sum (Consecutive)** | O(n) | O(n) | When events must be in a **contiguous block** (streak-based) |
| **Sort + Greedy (Free Selection)** | O(n log n) | O(1) or O(n) | When events are **independent** and any subset can be chosen |
| **Min-Heap of size k (Free Selection)** | O(n log k) | O(k) | Streaming data or when $k \ll n$ |

**When to Use Which:**
- **Use Prefix Sum** when the problem says "k **consecutive** events" or "k-day streak."
- **Use Sort + Greedy** when the problem says "any k events" with small n.
- **Use Heap** for large n with small k in real-time data streams. **(7 marks — 3 for code, 2 for comparison, 2 for selection guidance.)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in This Experiment:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `prefix[0] = 0` sentinel** — Causes off-by-one errors in the first window sum. **Penalty: -1 to -2 marks.**
> 2. **Using `prefix[k]` instead of `prefix[k] - prefix[0]`** — Works only by coincidence because `prefix[0] = 0`, but loses conceptual clarity marks. KTU examiners prefer the explicit form.
> 3. **No edge-case handling for `k > n`** — A frequent 1-mark deduction. Always include the guard clause.
> 4. **Confusing "at most k" with "exactly k"** — If the problem says "at most," returning a sum from fewer than k events (when all remaining are negative) is **correct** and earns full marks.
> 5. **O(n·k) brute force without prefix sums** — Loses marks on the "optimal approach" requirement. KTU explicitly tests whether you know O(n) is achievable.
> 6. **Not printing the window indices** in the output — Many KTU problems ask to also print *which* window gives the max. Always track the start index.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🎯 **Core Idea:** The maximum activity points problem is solved by either **prefix sums** (consecutive) or **sort + greedy** (non-consecutive).
- 📐 **Master Formula:** $\text{WindowSum}(i) = prefix[i+k] - prefix[i]$ is the **heart** of the consecutive-events variant.
- ⏱️ **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ for prefix-sum, $O(n \log n)$ for sort-based selection.
- 💾 **Space Complexity:** $O(n)$ for the prefix array; $O(1)$ in-place for the sort variant.
- 🔒 **Sentinel Rule:** Always initialize $prefix[0] = 0$ to make the window-sum formula uniform.
- 🛡️ **Edge Cases to Test:** All-negative arrays, `k = 1`, `k = n`, `k > n`, single-element arrays.
- 🧠 **Bloom's Taxonomy Level (KTU):** This experiment tests **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)** — you must implement the algorithm AND trace/modify it.
- 🔁 **Related KTU Experiments:** Maximum subarray sum (Kadane's), Two-sum problem, Sliding window maximum (uses deque).
- 💡 **Memory Aid:** Think **"PWS"** — **P**refix sum, **W**indow, **S**lide.
- 📊 **Trace Table is Mandatory:** In KTU viva, always show a 3-column trace (Window | Sum | MaxSoFar) for full marks.
- 🚫 **Don't Forget:** When the problem specifies "consecutive," the window must be **contiguous**; "at most k" does **not** mean "any k" — it means "≤ k, but the constraint structure depends on the variant stated."

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Merge K Sorted Lists Using a Min-Heap

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Merge K Sorted Lists** is a classic priority-queue based divide-and-conquer problem wherein **K** independently sorted linked lists (or arrays) are combined into a single globally sorted list of length $N = \sum_{i=1}^{K} n_i$. A **Min-Heap (Min-Priority Queue)** is used to repeatedly extract the smallest available front-element across all K lists in $O(\log K)$ time, yielding an overall time complexity of $O(N \log K)$.

Where:
- $K$ = Number of input sorted lists
- $n_i$ = Length of the $i^{th}$ list
- $N$ = Total number of elements across all lists
- The **heap property** enforces that the parent node is always $\leq$ its children (min-heap) or $\geq$ its children (max-heap).

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Triage Desk**

Imagine **K** hospital wards, each with its own line of patients (already sorted by arrival time, i.e., severity). The triage nurse at the central desk needs to call patients for surgery in globally correct order. The nurse cannot scan all wards every time (that would be $O(NK)$). Instead, she keeps a **small clipboard** showing only the **first patient from each ward** ($K$ entries). She always calls the sickest patient (smallest element) from the clipboard, then pulls the next patient from *that same ward* into the clipboard. This is exactly how a **min-heap** works — the clipboard is the heap holding $K$ "candidate" pointers, and each pop-push operation costs $O(\log K)$.

### Key Observations
- The naive pairwise merge costs $O(NK)$ in the worst case.
- The min-heap reduces this dramatically because we only compare $K$ candidates, not all $N$ elements.
- Standard **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics** used in this experiment:
  - **Time Complexity:** $O(N \log K)$
  - **Space Complexity:** $O(K)$ for the heap + $O(N)$ for the output list
  - **Comparator Cost:** Each `heappush` / `heappop` = $O(\log K)$

---

### Learning Outcomes Mapped (KTU 2024 Scheme)

| CO ID | Course Outcome | Bloom's Level |
|-------|----------------|---------------|
| **CO2** | Apply advanced data structures (heaps, priority queues) to solve real-world problems | Apply (L3) |
| **CO3** | Analyze time and space complexity of algorithms using asymptotic notation | Analyze (L4) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Heap Mechanism

The min-heap based merge operates on the following disciplined logic:

1. **Heap Initialization Phase**
   - Insert the **head element of each of the K lists** into the min-heap.
   - Heap size = $K$.
   - Cost of heapification: $O(K)$ using bottom-up heapify, or $O(K \log K)$ via repeated insertion.

2. **Extraction Phase (Repeats N times)**
   - Pop the **minimum element** $x$ from the heap root. (Cost: $O(\log K)$)
   - Append $x$ to the result list.
   - If the list from which $x$ was drawn has more elements, **push the next head** into the heap. (Cost: $O(\log K)$)

3. **Termination**
   - The heap empties when all $N$ elements have been popped and emitted.
   - Result list is fully sorted in ascending order.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a min-heap and not a sorted array or BST?**
> A sorted array would force $O(K)$ insertion per step (re-shifting). A BST gives $O(\log K)$ average but $O(K)$ worst-case. The **binary heap guarantees $O(\log K)$** in the worst case and uses contiguous array storage, making it cache-friendly.

## 2.2 Data Structure Used Per Node

Each heap node is typically a **tuple** (or custom struct/class) carrying:

- `val` — the current data value
- `list_index` — which original list this element came from
- `element_index` — the position inside that list (or a pointer to the next node)

Python's `heapq` module uses a **tuple comparison** that automatically compares the first element (`val`), breaking ties using the second (`list_index`). This is the trick that prevents duplicate values from being ambiguously compared.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Complexity | Unit / Notes |
|---------------|------------|------------|--------------|
| $K$ | Number of input sorted lists | Input parameter | dimensionless |
| $n_i$ | Length of the $i^{th}$ list | Input parameter | elements |
| $N$ | Total elements $= \sum n_i$ | Derived | elements |
| Heap Initialization | Build heap of K front elements | $O(K)$ | using heapify |
| `heappop` per element | Extract minimum | $O(\log K)$ | comparisons |
| `heappush` per element | Insert next candidate | $O(\log K)$ | comparisons |
| **Total Time** | $N$ extractions + $N$ insertions | $O(N \log K)$ | worst case |
| **Auxiliary Space** | Heap storage | $O(K)$ | pointers + values |
| **Output Space** | Result list | $O(N)$ | mandatory |
| Stable Sort Property | Tie-breaking by list order | Preserved if tie-breaker added | Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical formula for board exams:** The total time complexity is always written as $O(N \log K)$, **never** $O(N \log N)$. The heap size remains $K$, not $N$, throughout the algorithm. This is a very common KTU exam trap.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **External Sorting (Disk-based):** When datasets exceed RAM, K-way merge with a K-way min-heap is the standard merge phase of external merge sort used in databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL).
- **Stream Merging in Log Aggregators:** Tools like Splunk, Elasticsearch use K-way heap merges to combine sorted log shards.
- **Network Packet Scheduling:** QoS routers merge K sorted priority queues.
- **Hadoop/MapReduce Shuffle Phase:** Reduce phase uses K-way merge across mapped partitions.
