# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Manual Trace

Let us trace the algorithm on:

$$\text{L1} = [1, 4, 7], \quad \text{L2} = [2, 5, 8], \quad \text{L3} = [3, 6, 9]$$

So $K = 3$ and $N = 9$.

### Step 0 — Heap Initialization

Insert the first element of each list into the heap. The heap is a min-heap on `val`:

| Heap State (min-heap array) | Min-Element | List Source |
|-----------------------------|-------------|-------------|
| $[(1, 0), (2, 1), (3, 2)]$ | 1 | L1 |
| $[(2, 1), (3, 2)]$          | 2 | L2 |
| $[(3, 2)]$                  | 3 | L3 |

Each tuple stores `(val, list_index)`. Result so far: `[]`.

### Step-by-Step Extraction Trace

| Step | Pop From Heap | Append to Result | Push Next From List | Heap After Push | Result |
|------|---------------|------------------|---------------------|------------------|--------|
| 1 | `(1, 0)` → 1 | [1] | L1 → 4 | `[(2,1), (3,2), (4,0)]` | [1] |
| 2 | `(2, 1)` → 2 | [1, 2] | L2 → 5 | `[(3,2), (4,0), (5,1)]` | [1, 2] |
| 3 | `(3, 2)` → 3 | [1, 2, 3] | L3 → 6 | `[(4,0), (5,1), (6,2)]` | [1, 2, 3] |
| 4 | `(4, 0)` → 4 | [1, 2, 3, 4] | L1 → 7 | `[(5,1), (6,2), (7,0)]` | [1, 2, 3, 4] |
| 5 | `(5, 1)` → 5 | [1..5] | L2 → 8 | `[(6,2), (7,0), (8,1)]` | [1..5] |
| 6 | `(6, 2)` → 6 | [1..6] | L3 → 9 | `[(7,0), (8,1), (9,2)]` | [1..6] |
| 7 | `(7, 0)` → 7 | [1..7] | L1 → None | `[(8,1), (9,2)]` | [1..7] |
| 8 | `(8, 1)` → 8 | [1..8] | L2 → None | `[(9,2)]` | [1..8] |
| 9 | `(9, 2)` → 9 | [1..9] | L3 → None | `[]` | [1..9] |

**Final Sorted Output:** $[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9]$. Heap empties exactly at step 9.

---

## 3.2 Complexity Derivation (Exhaustive)

Let $T(N, K)$ be the total running time. We decompose:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(N, K) &= \underbrace{O(K)}_{\text{initial heap build}} + \underbrace{N \cdot \big[\, O(\log K) + O(\log K) \,\big]}_{\text{N extractions + N insertions}} \\[6pt]
&= O(K) + O(N \log K) \\[6pt]
&= O(N \log K) \quad \text{(since } N \geq K \text{ in the non-trivial case)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The **$O(K)$ initialization term is dominated** by $O(N \log K)$ as long as $N \geq 1$, which is always true. Hence the KTU board answer is $O(N \log K)$.

**Space Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{aux}} &= \underbrace{O(K)}_{\text{heap}} + \underbrace{O(1)}_{\text{scratch variables}} = O(K) \\
S_{\text{output}} &= O(N) \quad \text{(result list — not counted as auxiliary)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Approach | Time Complexity | Space | Stability | Practical Use |
|----------|----------------|-------|-----------|---------------|
| Naive (concatenate & sort) | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N)$ | Depends on sort | Small K, unsorted input |
| Pairwise merge (sequential) | $O(NK)$ | $O(N)$ | Yes | Streaming |
| **Min-Heap K-way merge** | $\mathbf{O(N \log K)}$ | $\mathbf{O(K)}$ | **Yes** | **Best general-purpose** |
| Tournament Tree (loser tree) | $O(N \log K)$ | $O(K)$ | Yes | External sort, fewer comparisons |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** When $K$ is small (say $\leq 16$), the constant factor of the heap is comparable to simpler methods, but for large $K$ (e.g., $K = 1000$ in distributed systems), $O(N \log K)$ massively outperforms $O(NK)$.

---

## 3.4 Pseudocode (Algorithm Specification — for record purposes)

```text
ALGORITHM MergeKSortedLists(lists[1..K]):
    minHeap ← empty priority queue
    FOR i FROM 1 TO K:
        IF lists[i] is not empty:
            minHeap.push( (lists[i].head.value, i, lists[i].head) )
    result ← empty list

    WHILE minHeap is not empty:
        (val, i, node) ← minHeap.pop()
        result.append(val)
        IF node.next is not null:
            minHeap.push( (node.next.value, i, node.next) )

    RETURN result
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The tuple comparison `(val, i, node)` is the cornerstone — Python's `heapq` (and most comparator-based heaps) compares element-by-element, so adding `i` as a tie-breaker guarantees a strict total order and **prevents comparison errors when duplicate values** exist across different lists.

```mermaid
    CheckSource --> ExtractMin: No

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Receive K Lists] --> Init[Initialize Empty Heap]
    Init --> Loop[For each list i = 1..K]
    Loop --> Check{List i Empty?}
    Check -- Yes --> Skip[Skip this list]
    Check -- No --> Push[Push head into heap]
    Skip --> Loop
    Push --> Loop
    Loop --> Done[All lists processed]
    Done --> Merge[Enter Merge Phase]

    Empty -- No --> Extract[Pop min, push successor]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Why is a min-heap preferred over a max-heap for merging K sorted lists into a single ascending sorted list?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **min-heap** is preferred because it always places the **smallest available front-element** at the root. At each step we need to extract the globally smallest candidate across all K list-heads to maintain ascending order. A max-heap would always yield the largest element, producing a descending output (which would then need reversal). Since both heap variants have identical $O(\log K)$ operations, min-heap gives us the output **directly in the required order** without any post-processing. **(3 marks: 1 for identifying root property, 1 for ascending order, 1 for no post-processing needed)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**What is the role of storing the `list_index` along with the value in each heap node? What happens if it is omitted?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `list_index` serves as a **tie-breaker** when two lists have equal head values. It also enables the algorithm to **locate the source list** and push the next element from that same list after extraction. If omitted and the comparator compares only `val`, ties cause **ambiguous comparison errors** (TypeError in Python's `heapq` when types differ, or undefined ordering in C++). Storing `list_index` enforces a **strict total ordering** and ensures correctness with duplicate values. **(3 marks: 1 for tie-breaking, 1 for source tracking, 1 for strict ordering justification)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Most students **incorrectly write** the time complexity as $O(N \log N)$ instead of $O(N \log K)$. This single error costs **2 full marks**. Always remember: the heap size is $K$, not $N$. Also, do not forget to mention the **auxiliary space $O(K)$ separately from output space $O(N)$** — many students conflate the two and lose 1 mark.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the algorithm to merge **K sorted linked lists** into a single sorted list using a **min-heap**. Clearly state the data structure used at each heap node, the heap operations performed, and justify why each operation has complexity $O(\log K)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For $K = 3$ lists: $L_1 = [2, 6, 8]$, $L_2 = [3, 7, 10]$, $L_3 = [1, 4, 9]$, **manually trace** the algorithm step-by-step showing the heap state after each extraction. State the final sorted output and compute the total number of heap operations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

**Algorithm (7 marks):**

1. **Node structure:** Each heap entry stores a tuple `(val, list_index, node_pointer)`. The comparator orders by `val` first, then `list_index`. **(1 mark)**

2. **Heap initialization:** Traverse all K lists, push the head of each non-empty list into the min-heap. Heap size = $K$ (or fewer if some lists are empty). Cost = $O(K)$. **(1 mark)**

3. **Main loop:** While the heap is non-empty: **(1 mark)**
   - `heappop` the minimum element $\rightarrow O(\log K)$.
   - Append `val` to the result list.
   - If the popped node has a `next` pointer, `heappush` the successor $\rightarrow O(\log K)$.

4. **Justification of $O(\log K)$:** A binary heap of $n$ elements has height $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$. Sift-up / sift-down traverses at most one level per comparison, hence at most $\log_2 K$ swaps. With $K$ elements in our heap, this is $O(\log K)$. **(1 mark)**

5. **Termination:** Heap becomes empty when all $N$ elements have been emitted. **(1 mark)**

6. **Total complexity:** $N$ pop-push pairs $\Rightarrow O(N \log K)$ time, $O(K)$ auxiliary space. **(1 mark)**

7. **Output:** The result list is a single linked list (or array) of length $N$ in ascending order. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Trace (7 marks):**

Initial heap (after pushing 2, 3, 1):

| Step | Popped (val, src) | Result List | Heap After Push (if any) | Heap State |
|------|-------------------|-------------|--------------------------|------------|
| 0 | — | [] | Push 2(L1), 3(L2), 1(L3) | `[(1,2), (2,0), (3,1)]` |
| 1 | (1, 2) | [1] | Push 4 from L3 | `[(2,0), (3,1), (4,2)]` |
| 2 | (2, 0) | [1, 2] | Push 6 from L1 | `[(3,1), (4,2), (6,0)]` |
| 3 | (3, 1) | [1, 2, 3] | Push 7 from L2 | `[(4,2), (6,0), (7,1)]` |
| 4 | (4, 2) | [1, 2, 3, 4] | Push 9 from L3 | `[(6,0), (7,1), (9,2)]` |
| 5 | (6, 0) | [1, 2, 3, 4, 6] | Push 8 from L1 | `[(7,1), (8,0), (9,2)]` |
| 6 | (7, 1) | [1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7] | Push 10 from L2 | `[(8,0), (9,2), (10,1)]` |
| 7 | (8, 0) | [1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8] | L1 exhausted | `[(9,2), (10,1)]` |
| 8 | (9, 2) | [1..8, 9] | L3 exhausted | `[(10,1)]` |

- Each row: **1 mark** for correctly identifying popped value, **0.5 mark** for heap state, **0.5 mark** for result list. **Total ≈ 7 marks.**
- **Final output:** $[1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10]$ **(½ mark)**
- **Total heap operations:** 3 pushes (init) + 9 pops + 7 pushes (successors) = **19 heap operations** **(½ mark)**

> Note: An extra step marking may be awarded for showing heap internal array state.

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Compare the **naive approach** (concatenate all lists and sort) with the **min-heap approach** for merging K sorted lists. Use a tabular comparison covering time, space, stability, and a specific scenario where each excels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Implement the **min-heap based merge** algorithm in Python. Your implementation must: (i) handle empty input lists, (ii) handle K = 0 edge case, (iii) use `heapq` module, (iv) include comments explaining each heap operation. Also write the **dry-run** for input $L_1 = [5, 10]$, $L_2 = [1, 7, 11]$, $L_3 = [3]$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 mark per relevant row + 1 mark for overall conclusion):**

| Criterion | Naive (Concatenate + Sort) | Min-Heap K-way Merge |
|-----------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| Time complexity | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N \log K)$ |
| Space complexity | $O(N)$ | $O(K)$ auxiliary + $O(N)$ output |
| Stability | Depends on sort algorithm | Guaranteed (with tie-breaker) |
| Best case | $K = 1$ or unsorted input | $K \geq 2$ sorted input |
| Heap dependency | None | Requires min-heap |
| Practical for huge $N$ | Slower for large $N$, large $K$ | Excellent — near-linear in $N$ |
| Online/streaming | Not suitable | Suitable (process one element at a time) |

**Scenario where naive excels (1 mark):** When $K$ is very large and $N$ is small, the overhead of building/maintaining a heap may exceed the cost of a single $O(N \log N)$ sort. Also when lists are **not pre-sorted**, the heap approach is invalid.

**Scenario where heap excels (1 mark):** When $K$ is moderate (10–1000) and the input lists are already sorted, $O(N \log K) \ll O(N \log N)$.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Python Implementation (5 marks):**

```python
import heapq

def merge_k_sorted_lists(lists):
    """
    Merge K sorted lists into a single sorted list using a min-heap.
    
    Time:  O(N log K) where N = total elements, K = number of lists
    Space: O(K) auxiliary + O(N) for output
    """
    # Edge case: empty input
    if not lists or all(len(lst) == 0 for lst in lists):
        return []
    
    min_heap = []
    
    # Step 1: Push head of each non-empty list into the heap
    # Each entry is (value, list_index, element_index)
    for i, lst in enumerate(lists):
        if lst:  # skip empty lists
            heapq.heappush(min_heap, (lst[0], i, 0))
    
    result = []
    
    # Step 2: Repeatedly extract min and push successor
    while min_heap:
        val, list_idx, elem_idx = heapq.heappop(min_heap)
        result.append(val)
        
        # Try to push the next element from the same list
        if elem_idx + 1 < len(lists[list_idx]):
            next_val = lists[list_idx][elem_idx + 1]
            heapq.heappush(min_heap, (next_val, list_idx, elem_idx + 1))
    
    return result


# Driver / Test
if __name__ == "__main__":
    L1 = [5, 10]
    L2 = [1, 7, 11]
    L3 = [3]
    print(merge_k_sorted_lists([L1, L2, L3]))  # [1, 3, 5, 7, 10, 11]
```

**Code Marking Scheme:**
- Correct `import heapq` and function signature: **1 mark**
- Heap initialization loop with empty-list guard: **1 mark**
- Main `while min_heap:` loop with `heappop` + `heappush`: **1.5 marks**
- Empty input edge case handled: **0.5 mark**
- Comments explaining each heap operation: **1 mark**

**Dry Run for $L_1 = [5,10], L_2 = [1,7,11], L_3 = [3]$ (2 marks):**

| Step | Popped | Result | Heap After Push | Heap Array |
|------|--------|--------|------------------|------------|
| 0 | — | [] | Push 5(L1), 1(L2), 3(L3) | `[(1,1,0), (3,2,0), (5,0,0)]` |
| 1 | (1, 1, 0) | [1] | Push 7 from L2 | `[(3,2,0), (5,0,0), (7,1,1)]` |
| 2 | (3, 2, 0) | [1, 3] | L3 exhausted | `[(5,0,0), (7,1,1)]` |
| 3 | (5, 0, 0) | [1, 3, 5] | Push 10 from L1 | `[(7,1,1), (10,0,1)]` |
| 4 | (7, 1, 1) | [1, 3, 5, 7] | Push 11 from L2 | `[(10,0,1), (11,1,2)]` |
| 5 | (10, 0, 1) | [1, 3, 5, 7, 10] | L1 exhausted | `[(11,1,2)]` |
| 6 | (11, 1, 2) | [1, 3, 5, 7, 10, 11] | L2 exhausted | `[]` |

**Final Output:** `[1, 3, 5, 7, 10, 11]` **(½ mark)** + Heap-empty termination noted **(½ mark).**

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Board Exam Focus**

- ✅ **Time complexity is $O(N \log K)$, NOT $O(N \log N)$.** Heap size = $K$, not $N$. This is the single most-tested fact.
- ✅ **Space complexity** = $O(K)$ auxiliary + $O(N)$ output. State both separately.
- ✅ **Heap node structure** = tuple `(value, list_index, element_index)`. The `list_index` is a mandatory tie-breaker.
- ✅ **Python's `heapq`** is a **min-heap** by default. Use `heapq.heappush` and `heapq.heappop`.
- ✅ **Algorithm invariants:**
  - Heap always contains at most $K$ elements (one candidate per list).
  - Heap root is the smallest available front-element across all lists.
  - Each iteration emits exactly one element to the output.
- ✅ **Edge cases to handle in viva/lab:**
  - Empty input → return `[]`.
  - Some lists empty → skip during init (heap will have < K elements).
  - All lists empty → return `[]`.
  - Single list ($K=1$) → return that list directly (heap of size 1 is trivial).
  - Duplicate values across lists → tie-breaker by `list_index` ensures stability.
- ✅ **Why not merge pairwise?** Sequential pairwise merging takes $O(NK)$ because the final merge has to process $N$ elements from an already-large list.
- ✅ **Comparison with merge sort:** K-way heap merge is the **merge step of generalized merge sort** where the partition is into $K$ parts instead of 2.
- ✅ **Practical engineering link:** External merge sort in databases (PostgreSQL, Oracle) uses **loser trees** (a tournament-tree variant) which is an optimization of the min-heap with fewer comparisons per operation.
- ✅ **Lab record must include:** Algorithm, dry-run trace, complexity derivation, full code, and output screenshot.
